ref: Later, Leah had a daughter and named her Dinah.
text: Na uwi ɔdi kama ni, Lea úbawɔ ulekubi úte Yakob na ɔ́sia-nwu lɛnyi ɔnii Dina.

ref: Finally, God remembered Rachel—he answered her prayer by giving her a son. “God has taken away my disgrace,” she said.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Unwoti Katɔti ɔ́ya na Rahel ɔ́ti ka útayiƒala-nwu. Anwuɔsu ɔ́lɛmi-nwu kaƒɔ, na ɔ́wɔ ɔnanabi. Na ɔ́su ɔnini, “Unwoti Katɔti údie kɔƒa mina anumi.”

ref: “I'll name the boy Joseph, and I'll pray that the Lord will give me another son.” Jacob and Laban
text: Na ɔ́sia ubi ɔmmɔ lɛnyi ɔnii Yoseƒ, na nwuna kalɔ ndi sɔ ti bunku. Na ɔ́su ɔnini, “Muuvele Unwoti Katɔti nnii úti ɔnanabi ɔbambadi ubunku ɔmɛni ute-mi.” Yakob Bebuo Bémomo Bébu

ref: After Joseph was born, Jacob said to Laban, “Release me from our agreement and let me return to my own country.
text: Ka Rahel ɔ́wɔ Yoseƒ kama ni, Yakob ɔ́su Laban ɔnini, “Die-mi ulu na lɛsɔ linwini nteesie mina kalɔ lɛlɔ.

ref: You know how hard I've worked for you, so let me take my wives and children and leave.”
text: Na eti mina beleku ku bebi ete-mi. Ɔsika bana lili na lɛ́bla kabla líte-ɔ. Na ɔlɛka lɛyu ulu. Agyi kabla kani udu na lɛ́bla lite-ɔ.”

ref: Jacob answered: You've seen how hard I've worked for you, and you know how your flocks and herds have grown under my care.
text: Mmɔ na Yakob ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Agyi kalɛ na lɛ́bla kabla líte-ɔ ku kalɛ na ƒula bebuo bádia-a na kalɛ na mɔɔnu-ma lɛlɔ ɔsu.

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Bebuo ɛlɛɛ bani na ebo asa na líbo ni, béemomo geen. Na Unwoti Katɔti kusɛ kusɛ ɔ́siaku lɛla biala lɛni na lɛ́bla líte-ɔ. Nsu Ɔƒɛni ni, uwi ɔmani na ami lɛnɔ ndubɔbla lɛla lɛdi lite lɛlɔ na meti mada mina kaduli?”

ref: “How much do you want me to pay you?” Laban asked. Then Jacob told him: I don't want you to pay me anything. Just do one thing, and I'll take care of your sheep and goats.
text: Amu na Laban úye-nwu ɔnini, “Bi na ɔlɛka lite-ɔ?” Mmɔ ni, Yakob údie kenye ɔnini, “Tete-mi lɛla lɛdi. Nsu si adubɔkana na abla lɛla linwi lɛni na meye-ɔ mɛni ete-mi ni, ndubusie anumi na ntaanu-ɔ bebuo lɛlɔ na nteesienku-ma kadikɔ lɛnɔ.

ref: Let me go through your flocks and herds and take the sheep and goats that are either spotted or speckled and the black lambs. That's all you need to give me.
text: Ya na lɛvi ƒula bebuo ƒui akɔ na lɛƒuna ɛlɛɛ bani naaadi ɔƒualadi sɔɔn ku baƒonubi ku besebi batondi lidie-ma akɔ. Na liti ama linwini mina lɛyuƒɔla.

ref: In the future you can easily find out if I've been honest. Just look and see if my animals are either spotted or speckled, or if the lambs are black. If they aren't, they've been stolen from you.
text: Na kalɛ na mɔɔdi ƒula etemila lɛlɔ ni, ɔdubudie lɛlɔ ɔtina uwi biala. Ɔsika si ébo ábavi bebuo bani na éti áƒɔ-mi kayu mmɔ akɔ na badi badi baƒualadi sɔɔn na bana lɛlɔ lítebo lítakya ɔƒualadi ku ɔtondi iye linwini tonwu ni, na líyu-ɔ.”

ref: “I agree to that,” was Laban's response.
text: Amu na Laban lɛnɔ údie kenye ɔnini, “Lɛ́tunaku-ɔ ka bɔdubɔbla lɛni.”

ref: Before the end of the day, Laban had separated his spotted and speckled animals and the black lambs from the others and had put his sons in charge of them.
text: Anwuɔsu údie bebuo bagyani ku beledi bani naaadi baƒualadi sɔɔn úte nwuna bebi bananabi ka bataanu-ma lɛlɔ.

ref: Then Laban made Jacob keep the rest of the sheep and goats at a distance of three days' journey.
text: Na Laban ku nwuna bebi béti bana bebuo bésienku ɔgyu kakaaka básiku Yakob badi kudɔ ɔni mabla emeyi atɛ ulu busie. Na Yakob ɔkya kama ɔɔnu Laban bebuo bakladi lɛlɔ.

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Amu na Yakob úsie útékulo kugyii kɔgba kɔwɔdi blii kɔdi. Na ɔ́nwi evuo ɔ́mami kɔgba kɔmmɔ lɛƒɛnyi lɛdi. Na lɛƒɛnyi lɛdi úbu evuo ɔ́mana ɔsu ɔ́bla nwana nwana.

ref: Then he put the branches where the sheep and goats would see them while they were drinking from the water trough. The goats mated there
text: Ɔmmɔ kama úti kɔgba kɔmmɔ útákya kesii kani na bebuo baaní ntu na kɔlɛkɛ kemmo.

ref: Some of the sheep that Jacob was keeping for Laban were already spotted. And when the others were ready to mate, he made sure that they faced in the direction of the spotted and black ones. In this way, Jacob built up a flock of sheep for himself and did not put them with the other sheep.
text: Na si báwɔ bebi bammɔ lɛni ni, Yakob uudie bani na bese nwana nwana mmɔ taasiku Laban bebuo bammɔ akɔ teese sɛsɛɛ. Na Laban bebuo bammɔ ni, ɔ́ya na kɔgba nwana nwana kɔmmɔ kɔtɛ-ma anumi uwi biala. Ulu ɔmɛni lɛlɔ na ɔ́sia na ɔ́kɛ baƒonu bamba bani neeebunku Laban badi.

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Na si uwi ɔni na bebuo bani mbo ɔlɔkplu baakɔ́ beledi bammɔ ni, ɔɔyá na baanu kɔgba nwana nwana kɔmmɔ kalɛ na si bawɔ ni, bebi bammɔ babla lɛni.

ref: But he would not put out the branches when the weaker animals were mating. So Jacob got all of the healthy animals, and Laban got what was left.
text: Na bebuo bani neeebo ɔlɔkplu ni, uléti kɔgba kɔmmɔ taakya bana ntu kanikɔ. Anwuɔsu si bákɔ lɛlɔ na báwɔ ni, balábla nwana nwana. Na bani neeese nwana nwana mmɔ na beeebo ɔlɔkplu lɛnɔ ndi Laban badi.

ref: Jacob soon became rich and successful. He owned many sheep, goats, camels, and donkeys, as well as a lot of slaves.
text: Na ɔmmɔ ɔsu Yakob ɔ́kɛ lɛkɛ pii. Nwuna bebuo bemomo. Na ubo kabla bablane bananabi ku belekubi pii lɛnɔ. Na nwuna bebuo ala betine bani mbo keƒu ku bani neeebo keƒu lɛnɔ bémomo.

ref: Jacob heard that Laban's sons were complaining, “Jacob is now a rich man, and he got everything he owns from our father.”
text: Uwi ɔmmɔ ka Laban bebi bananabi bammɔ bakya baatɛmi ni, Yakob únu. Na básu banini, “Yakob ɔ́ƒɔ bula ɔti ala ani nse-nwu ƒui únwini anwu ɔdi. Na akɛla pii ani ƒui na ubo mɛni ni, bula ɔti ala ani na nka ubo na adi.”

ref: Jacob also noticed that Laban was not as friendly as he had been before.
text: Na Yakob ɔlati ɔ́nu ɔ́ti ka Laban ɔbladu ɔni na ɔɔtina-nwu ni, ɔ́vumanaku kalɛ na úse kuuku.

ref: One day the Lord said, “Jacob, go back to your relatives in the land of your ancestors, and I will be with you.”
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Unwoti Katɔti ɔ́ya na Yakob ɔ́ti ɔnini, “Nwini teesie ƒula ɔti bene kalɔ lɛlɔ na ƒula ɔtɔ bekyuli kudɔ. Na ndubulieku-ɔ uwi biala.”

ref: Jacob sent for Rachel and Leah to meet him in the pasture where he kept his sheep,
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ni, Yakob ɔ́ya na bátálɔ nwuna beleku bani ndi Rahel ku Lea ka babasiaku-nwu na alakpi akɔ kesii kani na ɔda nwuna bebuo úsienku.

ref: and he told them: Your father isn't as friendly with me as he used to be, but the God my ancestors worshiped has been on my side.
text: Ka bébo ni, ɔ́su-ma ɔnini, “Lɛ́nu lɛ́ti ka bina ɔti ɔbladu na mina lɛlɔ ɔ́vumana. Na ɔsɔ uuuse aani kudeedi kalɛ. Nsu na ɔmmɔ ƒui kama ni, mina Katɔti ɔni ndi mina ɔti Unwoti Katɔti uliku-mi.

ref: years to buy your sheep and goats. During that time you kept changing my wages.
text: Amii alati bɛgyi kalɛ na líti mina ɔyikun ƒui lɛ́bla kabla líte bina ɔti.

ref: and that he keeps cheating me by changing my wages time after time. But God has protected me.
text: Nsu ɔ́nya-mi ɔ́kya na ɔ́vumana mina lɛyuƒɔla leevu sɔɔn. Ina ɔmmɔ ƒui ni, Unwoti Katɔti útaya na ɔbla-mi kalala kadi.

ref: When your father said the speckled sheep would be my wages, all of them were speckled. And when he said the spotted ones would be mine, all of them were spotted.
text: Si bina ɔti ɔ́su ɔnii bebuo bebi bani na báwɔ-ma na bese nwana nwana ndi mina lɛyuƒɔla ni, na bawɔ bani nse nwana nwana. Na si ɔ́su ɔnii bani na ɔƒualadi úbo ɔ́kya-ma lɛlɔ ndi ƒula lɛyuƒɔla ni, na bawɔ bani na ɔƒualadi úbo ɔkya-ma lɛlɔ.

ref: That's how God has taken sheep and goats from your father and given them to me.
text: Ɔmɛni ɔsu na Unwoti Katɔti ɔ́ƒɔ bina ɔti bebuo kɔsia kɔni lɛlɔ na ɔ́sia anu úte-mi.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: “Ka bana ɔwɔwi údu ni, lígye kɔna kɔdi. Na lɛ́nu ka betukpa bani mɔɔkɔ́ beledi bammɔ ƒui ni, bese nwana nwana iye ɔƒualadi úbo ɔkya-ma lɛlɔ.

ref: Then God's angel called me by name. I answered,
text: Katɔti ɔtu ɔmmɔ ɔ́kpali-mi ɔnini, ‘Yakob.’ Ami lɛnɔ lídie kenye nnii, ‘Buvele aanu-mi.’

ref: and he said, “Notice that all the rams are either spotted or speckled. I know everything Laban is doing to you,
text: Mmɔ na ɔ́su-mi ɔnini, ‘Talaku anumi katɔ na anu betukpa bani mɔɔkɔ́ bebuo bammɔ. Aanu ka bese nwana nwana na ɔƒualadi úbo ɔkya-ma lɛlɔ. Lɛ́nu lɛla lɛni na Laban ɔkya ɔɔblaku-ɔ.

ref: and I am the God you worshiped at Bethel, when you poured olive oil on a rock and made a promise to me. Leave here at once and return to the land where you were born.”
text: Ami ndi Unwoti Katɔti ɔni na ánu na Betel kesii kani na éti lɛtabi éku ényene aani lɛkpabi na éti nnɔmii éwi-ni lili. Mmɔ na ákpali-mi ése kenye kadi ése. Ɔƒaani ni, tɔlɔ kalɔ kamɛni lɛlɔ na eteesie ƒula kadi bekyuli kalɔ lɛlɔ.’ ”

ref: He treats us like foreigners and has even cheated us out of the bride price that should have been ours.
text: Ɔƒɛni ni, baƒɔ kani na ɔɔnu-bu. Use aani buye kani na úti-bu úye. Ɔsika kavumi kani na báƒɔ na bula lili ni, ɔ́ɔdi egulabi ammɔ ƒui.

ref: So do whatever God tells you to do. Even the property God took from our father and gave to you really belongs to us and our children.
text: Na sɔsɔ na ɔdi ka ala ani ƒui na Katɔti ɔ́ƒɔ ɔ́siku bula ɔti kudɔ ni, adi abu ku bula bebi adi. Anwuɔsu lɛla biala na Unwoti Katɔti ɔ́su-ɔ ni, bla lɛni kani.”

ref: Then Jacob, his wives, and his children got on camels and left
text: Ka bédie kenye kammɔ lɛni ni, Yakob ɔ́ya na nwuna beleku ku bebi bammɔ bákɔ bélie nwuna bebuo ala betine lɛlɔ.

ref: northern Syria for the home of his father Isaac in Canaan. Jacob took along all his flocks, herds, and other property.
text: Na ɔ́ya na ama ku nwuna bebuo ƒui bágyu bátɔlɔ. Na útiku nwuna ala ani ƒui na ɔ́kɛ uwi ɔni na ɔ́kya Mesopotamia kalɔ lɛlɔ mmɔ, na báyu ulu bésela Kanaan kalɔ lɛlɔ na ɔti Isak kudɔ.

ref: Before Rachel left, she stole the household idols while Laban was out shearing his sheep.
text: Uwi ɔni na Laban úsie útéteekulo baƒonu bammɔ linwini ni, Rahel úyu ɔti kaduli baƒiɔ badi.

ref: When Jacob crossed the Euphrates River and headed for the hill country of Gilead, he took with him everything he owned. Laban Catches Up with Jacob
text: Yakob úkeliku nwuna ala ɔ́tɔlɔku ƒui. Na ka ɔ́via ketu ni, úti ɔ́kɔ úsie abi lɛlɔ na Gilead kalɔ lɛlɔ. Laban Ɔ́sia Yakob Kama

ref: Three days later Laban found out that Jacob had gone.
text: Ka Yakob ɔ́tɔlɔ limeyi lɛtɛdi ni, Laban úbenu ka bukeli na Yakob úkeli.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Mmɔ kani na ɔ́sala nwuna lɛlɔ bekyuli na báta básia-nwu kama. Emeyi maatɛ sɔɔn na básia-nwu kama ulu asa na bátánu-nwu na Gilead kalɔ lɛlɔ na abi lɛlɔ.

ref: But God appeared to Laban in a dream that night and warned, “Don't say a word to Jacob. Don't make a threat or a promise.”
text: Amu na Unwoti Katɔti údie lɛlɔ ɔ́tina Laban na kɔna kemmo na usientɛ. Na ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Nu lɛlɔ kemmo kaduɛ na alasu Yakob lɛla lɛdi. Likpidi o-o lɛbuni o-o ni, tasu-nwu lɛla lɛdi.”

ref: Jacob had set up camp in the hill country of Gilead, when Laban and his relatives came and set up camp in another part of the hill country. Laban went to Jacob
text: Na uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo ni, Yakob ɔ́ɔtu nwuna adi kevo na abi lɛlɔ na Gilead kalɔ lɛlɔ mmɔ. Na uwi ɔni na Laban ku anwu badi bávi bátáda-nwu ni, ama lɛnɔ bátu bana keliekɔ na mmɔ.

ref: and said: Look what you've done! You've tricked me and run off with my daughters like a kidnapper.
text: Mmɔ na Laban úye Yakob ɔnini, “Bi na abla-mi mɛni? Átɔlɔ-mi na éti-mi bebi ékeliku aani bekyuli bani na kekpo kéƒie-ma.

ref: Why did you sneak away without telling me? I would have given you a going-away party with singing and with music on tambourines and harps.
text: Bi ɔsu na ékeli átɔlɔ na bɔbɛ kemmo lɛni na átakada-mi? Na bi ɔsu na átasu-mi na lise-ɔ ulu ku ebiti lɛnu ku agyala?

ref: You didn't even give me a chance to kiss my own grandchildren and daughters goodbye. That was really foolish.
text: Na átaya na lɛkya mina bebi ku bawɔ kɔgbu na lite-ma kusɛ kusɛ na bana ulu busie kemmo. Kɔdɔlɔ ala na ábla mɛni.

ref: I could easily hurt you, but the God your father worshiped has warned me not to make any threats or promises.
text: Mbo ɔyikun ɔni na lɛbla-a kalala. Nsu kamaadi Unwoti Katɔti ɔni ndi ƒula ɔti Katɔti ɔ́su-mi na kɔna kemmo usientɛ ɔnini, ‘Nu lɛlɔ kemmo kaduɛ na alasu Yakob lɛla lɛdi. Likpidi o-o, lɛbuni o-o, tasu-nwu lɛla lɛdi.’

ref: I can understand why you were eager to return to your father, but why did you have to steal my idols?
text: Na ngyi ka aayɔ́ geen ka asɔ enwini esie ƒ'ɔti kesii. Nsu ni, bi ɔsu na ɔdi ka eyu-mi kaduli baƒiɔ atɔlɔku?”

ref: Jacob answered, “I left secretly because I was afraid you would take your daughters from me by force.
text: Ka Laban úye-nwu kenye kamɛni ni, Yakob údie kenye ɔnini, “Lɛ́yia-a nnii adubuƒie ƒula bebi belekubi aƒɔ-mi kudɔ na ɔkplu kemmo.

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Na ƒula baƒiɔ ama ni, si ánu ukyuli ɔdi na bula akɔ ka ɔda-ma ni, ukyuli ɔmmɔ ɔsɔ ulelie ngba. Anwuɔsu na bula ɔtɔ bekyuli bamɛni ƒui anumi ni, vi mina ala ƒui kemmo na anu si ƒula lɛla lɛdi lɛkya-nya akɔ. Na si ánu lɛla lɛdi ni, na eti-ni.” Na uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo ƒui ni, Yakob útati ɔnii ulekudi Rahel néyu ɔti baƒiɔ bammɔ.

ref: Laban searched the tents of Jacob, Leah, and the two servant women, but did not find the idols. Then he went to Rachel's tent.
text: Laban úduo Yakob kevo kemmo na ulu ɔni na úte-nwu kenye. Na úduo Lea ku nwuna kabla bablane inyɔ bammɔ ƒui nvo kemmo, nsu útanu lɛla lɛdi. Ka ɔ́si mmɔ ni, úbeduo Rahel kevo kemmo.

ref: She had already hidden them in the cushion she used as a saddle and was sitting on it. Laban searched everywhere and did not find them.
text: Nsu asa na úbedu mmɔ ni, Rahel úuti ɔti kaduli baƒiɔ bammɔ ɔ́kya liboku lɛdi kalɔ na ɔ́sɔ úlie-ni lɛlɔ. Na ani na ɔɔsia nwuna ebuo ala utine kama teelie. Na Laban ɔ́wia lɛla biala kemmo ɔ́nɔ na útanu lɛla lɛdi.

ref: Rachel said, “Father, please don't be angry with me for not getting up; I'm having my period.” Laban kept on searching, but still did not find the idols.
text: Mmɔ na Rahel ɔ́su ɔti ɔnini, “Ati, buvele nda-a, teti kɔnyi takya-mi ka ńtata ńtenye na ƒula anumi. Lɛ́ƒɛ kɔnu na ubugya ɔni muubo-mi lɛlɔ ɔsu na lɛ́bla lɛni.” Anwuɔsu Laban útuku ɔ́nɔ na útanu nwuna kaduli baƒiɔ bammɔ.

ref: Jacob became very angry and said to Laban: What have I done wrong? Have I committed some crime? Is that why you hunted me down?
text: Yakob úbeti kɔnyi ɔ́kya Laban kaduɛ ɔni na útete-nwu anumi munu munu. Na úye-nwu ɔnini, “Bi ndi lɛla lɛni na balábla ukyuli na ami lɛ́bla-a. Na bi likpidi na eedie-mi ɔsu na átu-mi laa abasiaku-mi mɛni?

ref: After searching through everything I have, did you find anything of yours? If so, put it here, where your relatives and mine can see it. Then we can decide what to do.
text: Na ka éduo-mi ala ƒui kemmo álɛ ni, bi ndi lɛla lɛni na ánu na lɛdi ƒula kaduli lɛdi? Ti lɛla lɛmmɔ baava ƒula bekyuli ku ami badi ƒui anumi mɛni na batɛ ami ku-ɔ litemi bɔnu.

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: Eƒiebuo ɔdi úteƒie ƒula ebuo ɔdi na liti nwuna ɔku mbatina-a kudii. Si ɔdi úbo ute lɛni muedi ami mɔɔƒɔ kayu kammɔ. Na si ɔdi lɛnɔ na béyu ni, eeye-mi kayu kammɔ na mɔɔƒɔ teete-ɔ usien ku ɔƒɛ.

ref: I sweated every day, and I couldn't sleep at night because of the cold.
text: Lɛni sɔɔn na úse na ami ku-ɔ ntɛ. Na línye ɔƒɛ lɛlɔ limeyi biala. Na usien kɔyɔ ɔsu ni, naaakana bɔlɛɛ muedi.

ref: If the fearsome God worshiped by Abraham and my father Isaac had not been on my side, you would have sent me away without a thing. But God saw my hard work, and he knew the trouble I was in, so he helped me. Then last night he told you how wrong you were. Jacob and Laban Make an Agreement
text: Na Unwoti Katɔti ɔni ndi mina ɔti lɛnɔ Katɔti na ɔdi Abraham Katɔti ɔsiaku-mi. Na bɔyɔ geen bɔni na mina ɔti Isak ɔ́yɔ Unwoti Katɔti lɛnɔ búdie-mi. Si ɔɔɔdi lɛni nka áya na lɛ́tɔlɔ enukplu. Nsu Unwoti Katɔti ɔ́nu mina kogyoklo ku lɛbibo. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu na ɔ́dɔnku-ɔ kamaadi usien.”

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “Leah and Rachel are my daughters, and their children belong to me. All these sheep you are taking are really mine too. In fact, everything you have belongs to me. But there is nothing I can do to keep my daughters and their children.
text: Mmɔ ni, Laban údie kenye ɔnini, “Beleku bamɛni ni, mina bebi na badi. Na bana bebi bamɛni lɛnɔ ni, mina bebi na badi. Na bebuo bamɛni ƒui mina bebuo na badi. Ala ani ƒui na aanu mɛni ni, ami mbo-nya. Nsu ɔmwi ni, bi na ndubɔbla ku bebi belekubi bamɛni iye bana bebi bani na báwɔ?

ref: So I am ready to make an agreement with you, and we will pile up some large rocks here to remind us of the agreement.”
text: Bo na aya na ami ku-ɔ bunyimi lɛla lɛdi. Na lɛla lɛmmɔ linwini lɛla lɛni makya ami ku-ɔ ntɛ aani lɛla lɛni medie ami ku-ɔ ƒui kenye.”

ref: After Jacob had set up a large rock,
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ni, Yakob úti lɛtabi lɛdi úku únyene mmɔ.

ref: he told his men to get some more rocks and pile them up next to it. Then Jacob and Laban ate a meal together beside the rocks.
text: Na ɔ́su nwuna lɛlɔ bekyuli ɔnini, “Bɛla atabi bɛsala.” Na ama lɛnɔ bála atabi adi básala lisu. Na bélie mmɔ bádi na atabi lisu lɛmmɔ kudɔ.

ref: Laban named the pile of rocks Jegar Sahadutha. But Jacob named it Galeed.
text: Na kesii kani na bélie bádi mmɔ na Laban ɔ́sia lɛnyi ɔnii Yega Sahaduta. Na Yakob anwu ɔ́sia mmɔ lɛnyi ɔnii Gal-Ed.

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “This pile of rocks will remind us of our agreement.” That's why the place was named Galeed.
text: Amu na Laban ɔ́su ɔnini, “Atabi lisu lɛmɛni ndi likposela lɛni nkya ami ku-ɔ ntɛ.” Ɔmɛni ɔsu baalɔ mmɔ banii Gal-Ed na nwuna kalɔ ndi atabi lisu lɛni muudie kenye.

ref: Laban also said, “This pile of rocks means that the Lord will watch us both while we are apart from each other.” So the place was also named Mizpah.
text: Na basɔ baalɔ mmɔ Mispa. Ɔsika Laban ɔ́su ɔnini, “Unwoti Katɔti ɔlati ndubɔsia bula ƒui anu si búgyingyaku lɛlɔ ɔ́si mɛni.

ref: Then Laban said: If you mistreat my daughters or marry other women, I may not know about it, but remember, God is watching us!
text: Si átada mina bebi belekubi kaduɛ iye ásɔ ététi beleku bamba na bana kama ni, Unwoti Katɔti ɔɔnu. Ina ka ukyuli bamba ɔdi uuubunku-bu na mɛni ni, Unwoti Katɔti ndi ami ku-ɔ ƒui kenye udiene.”

ref: Both this pile of rocks and this large rock have been set up between us as a reminder. I must never go past them to attack you, and you must never come past them to attack me.
text: Naaavia atabi lisu lɛmɛni neeebo ƒula kakɛ nnii mbekpoku-ɔ. Na awɔ lɛnɔ alavia atabi lisu lɛmɛni iye lɛkpa lɛmɛni elebo-mi kakɛ anii ebekpo-mi.

ref: My father Nahor, your grandfather Abraham, and their ancestors all worshiped the same God, and he will make sure that we each keep the agreement. Then Jacob made a promise in the name of the fearsome God his father Isaac had worshiped.
text: Muuvele Abraham Unwoti Katɔti ku Nahor Unwoti Katɔti ɔni ndi bana bati Unwoti Katɔti ka ɔtɛ litemi biala nkya ami ku-ɔ ntɛ.” Ka ɔ́wɔlɔ ni, Yakob ɔ́kpali Katɔti ɔni na Isak ɔɔbu geen úse kenye kadi.

ref: Jacob killed an animal and offered it as a sacrifice there on the mountain, and he invited his men to eat with him. After the meal they spent the night on the mountain.
text: Na ɔ́bla akpala na kalɔ kammɔ lɛlɔ na abi lɛlɔ mmɔ. Na ɔ́lɔ anwu badi ƒui ka bebo babadi. Na ka bádi báwɔlɔ ni, bátɛ mmɔ.

ref: Early the next morning, Laban kissed his daughters and his grandchildren goodbye, then he left to go back home.
text: Na ka kadi kégye ɔtanta tutuutu ni, Laban ɔ́kya nwuna bawɔ ƒui kɔgbu ku nwuna bebi belekubi bammɔ. Na úte-ma kusɛ kusɛ, na únwini úsie ɔma.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Yakob lɛnɔ ɔ́sɔ úti nwuna ulu na ɔ́tɔlɔ. Na ulu lɛlɔ mmɔ ni, útásiaku Katɔti batu badi.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Uwi ɔni na ɔ́nu-ma kani na ɔ́su ɔnini, “Mɛni ndi Unwoti Katɔti kakyakɔ.” Ɔmmɔ ɔsu na ɔ́sia mmɔ lɛnyi ɔnii Mahanaim.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Yakob ɔ́tala batu badi ɔnii bagyu batɔ-nwu. Na ɔ́tala-ma ɔ́kya ɔnwani Esau na kalɛkɛ kani baalɔ Seir na Edom kalɔ kemmo.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Na ɔ́kya-ma litemi kenye ɔnini, “Bisie na bitásu mina ati Esau bɛnini, ‘Ami, ƒula kabla ɔblane Yakob, mɔɔyá na ati ka Laban kudɔ na lísie ńtákya uwi ɔni návi mɛni ƒui.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: Na mbo ɔmantɛ bakɔ ku bebuo ala betine ku baƒonu ku bese ku kabla bablane bananabi ku belekubi. Na ɔƒaani ni, mina Ati, mɔɔsia-a kenye kamɛni kalɛ na mina lɛlɔ lɛda-a ebiti.’ ”

ref: When the messengers returned, they told Jacob, “We went to your brother Esau, and now he is heading this way with
text: Ka batu bammɔ bénwini bébo ni, bábasu Yakob banini, “Búsie ƒula ɔnwani Esau kudɔ. Na anwu ɔlati ɔsia ulu ɔdubo ubasiaku-ɔ. Na ɔdanku nkyuli eti maane ubunku lɛlɔ.”

ref: Then Jacob prayed: You, Lord, are the God who was worshiped by my grandfather Abraham and by my father Isaac. You told me to return home to my family, and you promised to be with me and make me successful.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Yakob ɔ́kpali katɔ na úvele ɔnini, “O mina Unwoti, Abraham Katɔti ku Isak Katɔti, awɔ ɔni násu-mi anini, ‘Nwini na eteesie ƒula kalɔ lɛlɔ ku ƒula lɛlɔ bekyuli kudɔ na ndubɔya na enu ku akɔkɔ eyi-ɔ.’

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: Muuvele-ɔ nnii die-mi na lɛlɛ sɛɛn na mina ɔnwani Esau enu kemmo. Ɔsika mɔɔyia ka ɔdubo bɔta na ubekpoku-mi ku mina beleku ku bana bebi.

ref: But you have promised that I would be a success and that someday it will be as hard to count my descendants as it is to count the grains of sand along the seashore.
text: Nsu awɔ násu-mi anini, ‘Ndubɔnu ka enu ku akɔkɔ éyi-ɔ. Na ndubɔya na ƒula bebi ku bawɔ bemomo aani ɛpu kɔnwinimi. Na ukyuli ɔdi ulakana-ma bɔka.’ ”

ref: After Jacob had spent the night there, he chose some animals as gifts for Esau:
text: Yakob ɔ́tɛ mmɔ usien ɔmmɔ. Na ala ani na ɔda ɔkɛ ni, ɔ́ƒuna etela adi ka uti ute ɔnwani Esau.

ref: And Jacob told them to be sure to say that he was right behind them. Jacob hoped the gifts would make Esau friendly, so Esau would be glad to see him when they met.
text: Na bitayiƒala ka bɛsu-nwu bɛnini, ‘Ƒula kabla ɔblane Yakob ɔkpɛ kama ɔdubo.’ ” Yakob ɔ́su-ma lɛni ɔsika ɔ́bla kasusu ɔnini, “Ndubuti etela ani na líti lɛ́tualaku lɛtɔtɔ mɛni lɛyala-nwu kaƒɔ. Na ɔmmɔ kama si uwi ɔni na ami ku-nwu búbasa anumi ku anumi ni, ɔƒɛni kalɛ ɔdubo enu bugyingya na ɔƒɔ-mi.”

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ni, Yakob ɔ́sia mmɔ lɛnyi ɔnii Peniel, na ɔ́su ɔnini, “Lɛ́sia mɛni lɛnyi lɛni, ɔsika lɛ́nu Unwoti Katɔti anumi ku anumi na útayila-mi ngba.”

ref: Jacob put servants in charge of each herd and told them, “Go ahead of me and keep a space between each herd.”
text: Bebuo bamɛni ƒui na Yakob úti ɔ́kya nwuna kabla bablane lɛkɔkɔ ka bataanu-ma lɛlɔ bana esu esu ammɔ na ɔ́su-ma ɔnini, “Bɛgyu bataatɔ-mi na bɛya na kuƒi kɔkya bebuo bammɔ ntɛ na bana esu ammɔ kenye.”

ref: Then he said to the servant in charge of the first herd, “When Esau meets you, he will ask whose servant you are. He will want to know where you are going and who owns those animals in front of you.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, ɔ́su kabla ɔblane ɔni ntɔ ɔnini, “Si mina ɔnwani Esau úbasiaku-ɔ na úye ɔnini, ‘Ɔma kabla ɔblane na adi, na wɛ na ésela na ɔma mbo bebuo bani ntɔ-ɔ lɛtɔtɔ mmɔ’ ni,

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: na edie kenye anini, ‘Ƒula ɔnwani Yakob ɔni ndi-ɔ kabla ɔblane mbo-ma. Na badi etela ani na ɔnii nti mboku awɔ nwuna ati Esau. Ɔƒɛni ni, anwu ɔlati ɔkpɛ kama ɔdubo.’ ”

ref: Jacob also told the men in charge of the second and third herds and those who followed to say the same thing when they met Esau.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, ɔ́su nwuna kabla ɔblane unyɔdi ku ɔtɛdi ku bani ƒui mɔɔkpanku bebuo esu esu ammɔ ɔnini, “Si Esau úbasiaku-mi ni, na bɛsu-nwu lɛla lɛni kani na lɛ́su bina kabla ɔblane ɔkpana ɔni ntɔ mmɔ.

ref: Jacob's men took the gifts on ahead of him, but he spent the night in camp. Jacob's Name Is Changed to Israel
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu Yakob ɔ́ya na etela ammɔ ágyu átɔ. Nsu anwu ɔlati ɔ́ƒɔ lɛlɔ ɔ́tɛ mmɔ usien ɔmmɔ na kesii kani na ɔ́da keliekɔ mmɔ. Unwoti Katɔti Ɔ́vumana Yakob Lɛnyi

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: Ɔƒɛni Yakob lɛti kani nébu na ketu ɔvi ɔmɛni. Mmɔ na ɔnana ɔdi úbo úbekpoku-nwu laa útédu ka kadi kégye.

ref: When the man saw that he could not win, he struck Jacob on the hip and threw it out of joint.
text: Ka ɔnana ɔmmɔ ɔɔɔdaku tɔ Yakob lɛlɔ bɔkana bɔdi ni, ɔ́wɔ Yakob kasɔma likubi na líkpeli.

ref: They kept on wrestling until the man said, “Let go of me! It's almost daylight.” “You can't go until you bless me,” Jacob replied.
text: Mmɔ na ɔnana ɔmmɔ ɔ́su Yakob ɔnini, “Ya na nteesie, ɔsika kadi kéegye.” Ka ɔ́su-nwu lɛni ni, Yakob lɛnɔ udie kenye ɔnini, “Lɛɛɛdubo-ɔ kuƒi bute amu ntu éte-mi kusɛ kusɛ.”

ref: Then the man asked, “What is your name?” “Jacob,” he answered.
text: Amu na ɔnana ɔmmɔ úye-nwu ɔnini, “Dɛ na baalɔ-ɔ?” Mmɔ na Yakob údie kenye ɔnini, “Ami baalɔ Yakob.”

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Amu na ɔnana ɔmmɔ ɔ́su Yakob ɔnini, “Asɔ aladi lɛnyi anii Yakob. Mmomu bekyuli badubɔlɔ-ɔ banii Israel, ɔsika énye asiaku Unwoti Katɔti ku bekyuli na ádi lɛlɔ.”

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Mmɔ ni, Yakob ɔ́su ɔnini, “Buvele, lɔ ƒula lɛnyi te-mi.” Nsu ɔnana ɔmmɔ úye-nwu ɔnini, “Bɔsu na ada anii aaagyi ukyuli ɔni mɔɔtɛmiku-ɔ?” Na mmɔ ni, úte-nwu kusɛ kusɛ na kesii kani na benye mmɔ.

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Uwi ɔni na ɔkɛ Paniel mmɔ ni, kadi káata, na ɔkɛ ɔɔtu ɔgba taakya ka nwuna kasɔma likubi líkpeli ɔsu.

ref: and the muscle on his hip joint had been injured. That's why even today the people of Israel don't eat the hip muscle of any animal.
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ni, Israel kadi bekyuli balákun bebuo kasɔma likubi lɛna kpɛtɛ kpɛtɛ lɛni mma-nwu lɛlɔ mmɔ. Ɔsika mmɔ geen nékpeli Yakob mmɔ.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.

text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu Yakob ɔ́ya na etela ammɔ ágyu átɔ. Nsu anwu ɔlati ɔ́ƒɔ lɛlɔ ɔ́tɛ mmɔ usien ɔmmɔ na kesii kani na ɔ́da keliekɔ mmɔ. Unwoti Katɔti Ɔ́vumana Yakob Lɛnyi

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: Ɔƒɛni Yakob lɛti kani nébu na ketu ɔvi ɔmɛni. Mmɔ na ɔnana ɔdi úbo úbekpoku-nwu laa útédu ka kadi kégye.

ref: When the man saw that he could not win, he struck Jacob on the hip and threw it out of joint.
text: Ka ɔnana ɔmmɔ ɔɔɔdaku tɔ Yakob lɛlɔ bɔkana bɔdi ni, ɔ́wɔ Yakob kasɔma likubi na líkpeli.

ref: They kept on wrestling until the man said, “Let go of me! It's almost daylight.” “You can't go until you bless me,” Jacob replied.
text: Mmɔ na ɔnana ɔmmɔ ɔ́su Yakob ɔnini, “Ya na nteesie, ɔsika kadi kéegye.” Ka ɔ́su-nwu lɛni ni, Yakob lɛnɔ udie kenye ɔnini, “Lɛɛɛdubo-ɔ kuƒi bute amu ntu éte-mi kusɛ kusɛ.”

ref: Then the man asked, “What is your name?” “Jacob,” he answered.
text: Amu na ɔnana ɔmmɔ úye-nwu ɔnini, “Dɛ na baalɔ-ɔ?” Mmɔ na Yakob údie kenye ɔnini, “Ami baalɔ Yakob.”

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Amu na ɔnana ɔmmɔ ɔ́su Yakob ɔnini, “Asɔ aladi lɛnyi anii Yakob. Mmomu bekyuli badubɔlɔ-ɔ banii Israel, ɔsika énye asiaku Unwoti Katɔti ku bekyuli na ádi lɛlɔ.”

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Mmɔ ni, Yakob ɔ́su ɔnini, “Buvele, lɔ ƒula lɛnyi te-mi.” Nsu ɔnana ɔmmɔ úye-nwu ɔnini, “Bɔsu na ada anii aaagyi ukyuli ɔni mɔɔtɛmiku-ɔ?” Na mmɔ ni, úte-nwu kusɛ kusɛ na kesii kani na benye mmɔ.

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Uwi ɔni na ɔkɛ Paniel mmɔ ni, kadi káata, na ɔkɛ ɔɔtu ɔgba taakya ka nwuna kasɔma likubi líkpeli ɔsu.

ref: and the muscle on his hip joint had been injured. That's why even today the people of Israel don't eat the hip muscle of any animal.
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ni, Israel kadi bekyuli balákun bebuo kasɔma likubi lɛna kpɛtɛ kpɛtɛ lɛni mma-nwu lɛlɔ mmɔ. Ɔsika mmɔ geen nékpeli Yakob mmɔ.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Yakob lɛnɔ ɔ́sɔ úti nwuna ulu na ɔ́tɔlɔ. Na ulu lɛlɔ mmɔ ni, útásiaku Katɔti batu badi.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Uwi ɔni na ɔ́nu-ma kani na ɔ́su ɔnini, “Mɛni ndi Unwoti Katɔti kakyakɔ.” Ɔmmɔ ɔsu na ɔ́sia mmɔ lɛnyi ɔnii Mahanaim.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Yakob ɔ́tala batu badi ɔnii bagyu batɔ-nwu. Na ɔ́tala-ma ɔ́kya ɔnwani Esau na kalɛkɛ kani baalɔ Seir na Edom kalɔ kemmo.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Na ɔ́kya-ma litemi kenye ɔnini, “Bisie na bitásu mina ati Esau bɛnini, ‘Ami, ƒula kabla ɔblane Yakob, mɔɔyá na ati ka Laban kudɔ na lísie ńtákya uwi ɔni návi mɛni ƒui.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: Na mbo ɔmantɛ bakɔ ku bebuo ala betine ku baƒonu ku bese ku kabla bablane bananabi ku belekubi. Na ɔƒaani ni, mina Ati, mɔɔsia-a kenye kamɛni kalɛ na mina lɛlɔ lɛda-a ebiti.’ ”

ref: When the messengers returned, they told Jacob, “We went to your brother Esau, and now he is heading this way with
text: Ka batu bammɔ bénwini bébo ni, bábasu Yakob banini, “Búsie ƒula ɔnwani Esau kudɔ. Na anwu ɔlati ɔsia ulu ɔdubo ubasiaku-ɔ. Na ɔdanku nkyuli eti maane ubunku lɛlɔ.”

ref: Then Jacob prayed: You, Lord, are the God who was worshiped by my grandfather Abraham and by my father Isaac. You told me to return home to my family, and you promised to be with me and make me successful.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Yakob ɔ́kpali katɔ na úvele ɔnini, “O mina Unwoti, Abraham Katɔti ku Isak Katɔti, awɔ ɔni násu-mi anini, ‘Nwini na eteesie ƒula kalɔ lɛlɔ ku ƒula lɛlɔ bekyuli kudɔ na ndubɔya na enu ku akɔkɔ eyi-ɔ.’

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: Muuvele-ɔ nnii die-mi na lɛlɛ sɛɛn na mina ɔnwani Esau enu kemmo. Ɔsika mɔɔyia ka ɔdubo bɔta na ubekpoku-mi ku mina beleku ku bana bebi.

ref: But you have promised that I would be a success and that someday it will be as hard to count my descendants as it is to count the grains of sand along the seashore.
text: Nsu awɔ násu-mi anini, ‘Ndubɔnu ka enu ku akɔkɔ éyi-ɔ. Na ndubɔya na ƒula bebi ku bawɔ bemomo aani ɛpu kɔnwinimi. Na ukyuli ɔdi ulakana-ma bɔka.’ ”

ref: After Jacob had spent the night there, he chose some animals as gifts for Esau:

ref: The two servant women, Zilpah and Bilhah, together with their children went first, followed by Leah and her children, then by Rachel and Joseph.

ref: Jacob himself walked in front of them all, bowing to the ground seven times as he came near his brother.
