ref: But Esau ran toward Jacob and hugged and kissed him. Then the two brothers started crying.
text: Nsu Esau úkeli útásiaku Yakob na ɔ́kya-nwu kɔgbu na úbo enu ɔ́bala-nwu na anumi énye-ma ntu na lɛlɔyɔ ɔsu.

ref: When Esau noticed the women and children he asked, “Whose children are these?” Jacob answered, “These are the children the Lord has been kind enough to give to me, your servant.”
text: Na ka Esau ɔ́talaku anumi katɔ ni, ɔ́nu beleku bammɔ ku banamu bammɔ na úye Yakob ɔnini, “Ɔma bene nsiaku-ɔ mɛni?” Amu na údie kenye ɔnini, “Bebi bani na Unwoti Katɔti úte ami ƒula kabla ɔblane na lɛ́wɔ na nwuna lɛƒɔndia ɔsu na badi.”

ref: Then the two servant women and their children came and bowed down to Esau.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, kabla bablane belekubi bammɔ ku bana bebi báni béde na béboku eli kalɔ.

ref: Next, Leah and her children came and bowed down; finally, Joseph and Rachel also came and bowed down.
text: Na Lea ku nwuna bebi basia na ama lɛnɔ béboku eli kalɔ. Na kawɔlɔkɔ ni, Rahel ku Yoseƒ lɛnɔ bébeduo na ama lɛnɔ bákya eli kalɔ.

ref: Esau asked Jacob, “Why did you send those herds I met along the road?” “Master,” Jacob answered, “I sent them so you would be friendly to me.”
text: Ka báwɔlɔ lɛni ni, Esau úye Yakob ɔnini, “Bebuo esu esu pii ani na lɛsiaku mɛni ni, bi na adanku?” Mmɔ ni, Yakob údie kenye ɔnini, “Ati, buti na líti-ma muute-ɔ kalɛ na mina lɛlɔ lɛda-a ebiti.”

ref: “But, brother, I already have plenty,” Esau replied. “Keep them for yourself.”
text: Amu na Esau ɔ́su Yakob ɔnini, “Mbo ala pii kukuuku, anwuɔsu ya na awɔ adi lɛnɔ eteese-ɔ.”

ref: “No!” Jacob said. “Please accept them as a sign of your friendship for me. When you welcomed me and I saw your face, it was like seeing the face of God.
text: Nsu Yakob údie kenye ɔnini, “Buvele na nda-a, oowo! Si mina lɛlɔ lɛ́da-a ebiti bɔdi ni, amu ƒɔ mina etela ani na líti muute-ɔ. Ɔsika kalɛ na lɛda lɛlɛ-ɔ anumi na lɛnu-ɔ ni, use-mi aani kalɛ na lɛnu mina Unwoti Katɔti anumi. Anwuɔsu kalɛ na égyingya enu na áƒɔ-mi ku kayi ƒaa mɛni ni,

ref: Please accept these as gifts I brought to you. God has been good to me, and I have everything I need.” Jacob kept insisting until Esau agreed.
text: muuvele-ɔ nnii ƒɔ mina etela ani na lɛ́ya na béti béboku-ɔ. Ɔsika Unwoti Katɔti ɔ́bla-mi lɛƒɔndia na lɛla biala na mɔɔwia ni, mbo-ni.” Na ka Yakob unye kpakplaa lɛni ɔsu ni, Esau ɔ́ƒɔ etela ammɔ.

ref: “Let's get ready to travel,” Esau said. “I'll go along with you.”
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Esau ɔ́su Yakob ɔnini, “Ɔƒaani ni, ya na buteesie na lɛsiaku-ɔ kama.”

ref: But Jacob answered, “Master, you know traveling is hard on children, and I have to look after the sheep and goats that are nursing their young. If my animals travel too much in one day, they will all die.
text: Nsu Yakob ɔ́su Esau ɔnini, “Mina Ati, agyi ka bebi bammɔ bátabi tɔ lɛlɔ. Na ɔƒɛni lɛnɔ ɔlɛka lɛnu baƒonu ku ɔmantɛ bakɔ benidi bani nda bebi mmɔ lɛlɔ kaduɛ. Na si búkeliku-ma usi na búsie ulu umodi ɔmmɔ limeyi linwi ni, badubo bukpi ƒui.

ref: Why don't you go on ahead and let me travel along slowly with the children, the herds, and the flocks. We can meet again in the country of Edom.”
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu, mina ati, mɔɔyɔ́ si awɔ ágyu atɔ. Na lɛkpanku-ma nteeboku buɛɛ buɛɛ kalɛ na bebuo lisu lɛmmɔ badubɔkana busie kenye. Na lɛnu banamu bammɔ lɛnɔ na mbasiaku-ɔ na Seir kalɔ lɛlɔ.”

ref: Esau replied, “Let me leave some of my men with you.” “You don't have to do that,” Jacob answered. “I am happy, simply knowing that you are friendly to me.”
text: Mmɔ na Esau ɔ́su ɔnini, “Amu ya na lɛnyonu mina bekyuli badi lite-ɔ.” Amu na Yakob ɔnini, “Ati, bi ɔsu na ɔlɛka asɔ abla ɔmmɔ udu? Awɔ, si mina lɛlɔ lɛ́da-a kubiti kani ni, ɔ́bɔ.”

ref: But Jacob went to Succoth, where he built a house for himself and set up shelters for his animals. That's why the place is called Succoth. Jacob Arrives at Shechem
text: Na Yakob anwu ka bégyingyaku lɛlɔ ni, ɔ́tɔlɔ úsie kesii kani baalɔ Sukot na ɔ́tu nvo bɔni kemmo na anwu ku nwuna badi badubɔkya. Na ɔ́bla ayu úte nwuna bebuo lɛnɔ. Na ayu ani na ɔ́tu mmɔ ɔsu na baalɔ mmɔ ku ɔmwi ɔmɛni banii Sukot.

ref: After leaving northern Syria, Jacob arrived safely at Shechem in Canaan and set up camp outside the city.
text: Ka Yakob ɔ́tɔlɔ Mesopotamia kalɔ lɛlɔ mmɔ ni, úbeduo Sekem na Kanaan kalɔ lɛlɔ buɛɛ. Mmɔ ni, ɔ́sɔ ɔ́lɛ kevo bamba. Na si enye mmɔ ni, aanu Sekem ɔma unengudi ɔmmɔ teemene.

ref: The land where he camped was owned by the descendants of Hamor, the father of Shechem. So Jacob paid them
text: Na mmɔ ni, Yakob úti sika ɔƒualadi ebi eti anyɔ na úye kalɔ kadi. Mmɔ na ɔ́da keliekɔ. Na kalɔ kammɔ ni, úye-ka ɔ́siku Sekem ubidi Hamor bebi kudɔ.

ref: then he set up his tents and built an altar there to honor the God of Israel.
text: Na kalɔ kammɔ kemmo ni, ɔ́tu lɛkpabi na ɔ́sia-ni lɛnyi ɔnii El Elohe Israel.

ref: Dinah, the daughter of Jacob and Leah, went to visit some of the women who lived nearby.
text: Na uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo ni, Lea ubidi ugblebi ɔni baalɔ Dina ɔ́vi ɔgbami ɔnii utánu kalɔ kammɔ beleku.

ref: She was seen by Hamor's son Shechem, the leader of the Hivites, and he grabbed her and raped her.
text: Uwi ɔni na Hamor ubidi Sekem ɔni ntɔnku Hiwiƒuɔ ɔ́nu ulekubi ɔmɛni ni, úƒie-nwu na ɔ́yɔ-nwu mmɔ kani.

ref: But Shechem was attracted to Dinah, so he told her how much he loved her.
text: Ɔ́si ɔmmɔ lɛlɔ ni, Yakob ubidi Dina litemi lɛ́da Sekem kayi geen. Na ka ɔɔyɔ́-nwu ɔsu ni, ɔɔtɛmiku-nwu kpɛtɛ kpɛtɛ.

ref: Shechem even asked his father to arrange for him to marry her.
text: Na úsie utásu ɔti Hamor ɔnini, “Tévele ulekubi ɔmɛni te-mi na unwini-mi ulekudi.”

ref: Meanwhile, Jacob heard what had happened. But his sons were out in the fields with the cattle, so he did not do anything at the time.
text: Ɔƒɛni ka Yakob únu ka bébile ubidi ulekubi ni, útebimi kenye laa utédu ka nwuna bebi bananabi bási bébo, ɔsika béti bebuo bésienku kadikɔ.

ref: just as Jacob's sons were coming in from work. When they learned that their sister had been raped, they became furiously angry, because nothing is more disgraceful than rape, and it must not be tolerated.
text: Yakob bebi báasi alakpi akɔ bébeduo. Ka bénu lɛla lɛni nálɛ ni, béti kɔnyi geen na kɔƒa kubendi lɛla lɛni na Sekem ɔ́blaku bana ɔnwani Dina ka bátɛ ɔsu. Ɔsika ɔmɛni ɔdi lɛla lɛni na útalɛka nka ɔbla na Israel.

ref: Hamor said to Jacob and his sons: My son Shechem really loves Dinah. Please let him marry her.
text: Nsu Hamor ɔ́su-ma ɔnini, “Mina ubi Sekem ni, úti nwuna kayi ƒui ɔ́sia awɔ Yakob ubidi lɛlɔ. Anwuɔsu na buvele kemmo ni, ti ƒ'ubidi ulekubi ɔmɛni te-nwu na uti.

ref: Why don't you start letting your families marry into our families and ours marry into yours?
text: Na bɛya na buteeti lɛlɔ bebi. Biti bina belekubi bite-bu na buti na amii lɛnɔ biti abu badi.

ref: You can share this land with us. Move freely about until you find the property you want; then buy it and settle down here.
text: Bɛkana na bilie-bu akɔ, ɔsika kalɔ kewi kete-mii ƒaa. Bilie na bɛdi kuye na bɛbla akɛla pii bite lɛlɔ.”

ref: Shechem added, “Do this favor for me, and I'll give whatever you want.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Sekem ɔ́su Dina ɔti ku banwani ɔnini, “Bɛya na mina lɛlɔ lɛda-mii ebiti, na lɛla biala lɛni na bɛdubuye-mi ni, ndubɔbla lite-mii.

ref: Jacob's sons wanted to get even with Shechem and his father because of what had happened to their sister.
text: Na ka úubile Dina ɔsu ni, Yakob bebi bátɔlɔ Sekem ku ɔti Hamor na bédie kenye básu-ma lɛla lɛni mayɔ-ma kaƒɔ kani.

ref: So they tricked them by saying: You're not circumcised! It would be a disgrace for us to let you marry Dinah now.
text: Na básu-ma banini, “Bulakana lɛla lɛmmɔ udu bɔbla. Bulakana na buti bula ɔnwani bute kadi kani ukyuli nétekulo lego, ɔsika ɔdi-bu kɔƒa.

ref: But we will let you marry her, if you and the other men in your tribe agree to be circumcised.
text: Na lɛla lɛni kani maya na bunu ɔmɛni kalɔ ndi ka bina bebi bananabi ƒui babla aani kalɛ na buse. Na babla-ma lego bukulo ɔdi ɔni na bula ƒui bɔ́bla.

ref: Then your families can marry into ours, and ours can marry into yours, and we can live together like one nation.
text: Si bɛ́bla ɔmɛni ni, bɔdubute-mii bula belekubi na biti, na abu lɛnɔ buti amii badi. Bɔdubo-mii kudɔ bulie na bula ƒui bunwini bekyuli benwi.

ref: But if you don't agree to be circumcised, we'll take Dinah and leave this place.
text: Nsu si bilénu kalɔ bɛnii bekulo-mii ago bɔdi ni, bɔdubo bula ɔnwani bɔkpanku na bɔtɔlɔ-mii kalɔ lɛlɔ.”

ref: Hamor and Shechem liked what was said.
text: Bana kasusu kammɔ káda Sekem ku ɔti Hamor kubiti.

ref: Shechem was the most respected person in his family, and he was so in love with Dinah that he hurried off to get everything done.
text: Sekem ɔmɛni aani ubi unengudi kalɛ ni, útebile uwi ɔdi munu munu. Mmomu úsie utábla kalɛ kani na béte-ma kasusu, ɔsika ɔɔyɔ́ Yakob ubidi Dina geen.

ref: The two men met with the other leaders of their city and told them:
text: Anwuɔsu Hamor ku Sekem bésie ɔma ɔmmɔ ɔban kenye na bátátɛmiku bana ɔma banana.

ref: These people really are friendly. Why not let them move freely about until they find the property they want? There's enough land here for them and for us. Then our families can marry into theirs, and theirs can marry into ours.
text: Mmɔ na básu-ma banini, “Kadi kamɛni bekyuli badi bekyuli babuni na bese buɛɛ bete-bu. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu bɛya na belie-bu akɔ na bula kalɔ lɛlɔ na badi kuye. Kalɔ kewi na kadubudu ama lɛnɔ. Na bɔkana na buti bana beleku na ama lɛnɔ beti abu badi.

ref: We have to do only one thing before they will agree to stay here and become one nation with us. Our men will have to be circumcised just like theirs.
text: Nsu lɛla linwi kani na baawia taasiku-bu kudɔ asa na abu ku-ma kelie kammɔ kabla ɔbuni kawɔlɔ. Na lɛla lɛmmɔ ndi ka abu ku bula bananabi ƒui bukulo ago aani bana kalɛ.

ref: Just think! We'll get their property, as well as their flocks and herds. All we have to do is to agree, and they will live here with us.
text: Bilánu ka bana bebuo ku akɛla lɛnɔ adi abu lɛnɔ adi si búnu ɔmɛni kalɔ? Ɔmmɔ ɔsu bɛya na bunu ɔmɛni kalɔ na bakya-bu akɔ.”

ref: Every grown man followed this advice and got circumcised. Dinah's Brothers Take Revenge
text: Amu na bekyuli bani ƒui nésie ɔban kenye mmɔ bénu Hamor ku Sekem litemi lɛmmɔ kalɔ na ɔnana biala nkya ɔma unengudi ɔmmɔ kemmo úkulo lego.

ref: Three days later the men who had been circumcised were still weak from pain. So Simeon and Levi, two of Dinah's brothers, attacked with their swords and killed every man in the town,
text: Na emeyi atɛ kama ni, bana ago ani na békulo mmɔ lɛlɛ lɛmu lɛsia-ma na beenú ɔlɛ geen. Amu na Yakob bebi inyɔ Simeon ku Lewi bani ndi Diana banwani báta bétéduo-ma lɛlɔ ku kuwii. Na ka bekyuli bamɛni baaagyi beeese ɔsu ni, bebi bamɛni bébeyi-ma bádu ƒui na ɔma bonwu ɔmmɔ kemmo ɔni na banana bammɔ bása ƒui.

ref: including Hamor and Shechem. Then they took Dinah and left.
text: Bádu Hamor ku Sekem lɛnɔ na bákpanku bana ɔnwani Dina bátɔlɔku.

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Yakob bebi bábavi bekpine bammɔ lɛlɔ na bála bana ala ƒui batɔlɔku ɔma ɔni kemmo na bébile bana ɔnwani mmɔ.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Béƒie-ma bebuo lidu lidu ku bebuo ala betine ƒui bátɔlɔku. Na beti bana akɛla ani na ɔma kemmo ku mmo edula ƒui bátɔlɔku.

ref: After taking everything of value from the houses, they dragged away the wives and children of their victims.
text: Lɛni kani na béti bana beleku ku bebi ƒui aani ala ani na kekpo kéƒie bálɛku bana lɛtɔ ƒui.

ref: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “Look what you've done! Now I'm in real trouble with the Canaanites and Perizzites who live around here. There aren't many of us, and if they attack, they'll kill everyone in my household.”
text: Mmɔ na Yakob ɔ́su Simeon ku Lewi ɔnini, “Bíti lɛbibo bonwu bíboku-mi lɛlɔ. Na bíti lɛla libudi bibawɛɛ-mi lɛlɔ. Na ndubo buteenwenyi na Kanaanƒuɔ ku Perisiƒuɔ kadi bekyuli kudɔ na kalɔ kamɛni lɛlɔ. Bɛɛnu ka buuumomo na si básala lɛlɔ na báta kekpo na mina lɛlɔ ni, badubɔyila mina kaduli bekyuli ku ala ƒui udu.”

ref: They answered, “Was it right to let our own sister be treated that way?”
text: Amu na ama bédie kenye banini, “Na ábu anii bɔdubunye bɔnu na uti-bu ɔnwani ulekubi unwini ɔyalɛ na kadi kamɛni bekyuli akɔ?”

ref: God told Jacob, “Return to Bethel, where I appeared to you when you were running from your brother Esau. Make your home there and build an altar for me.”
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Unwoti Katɔti ɔ́su Yakob ɔnini, “Teesie Betel na etélie mmɔ. Na atu lɛkpabi na mmɔ ete Unwoti Katɔti ɔni nédie lɛlɔ ɔ́tina-a uwi ɔni na eekeli ƒ'ɔnwani Esau.”

ref: Jacob said to his family and to everyone else who was traveling with him: Get rid of your foreign gods! Then make yourselves acceptable to worship God and put on clean clothes.
text: Anwuɔsu Yakob ɔ́su nwuna kaduli bekyuli ku bani ƒui nkya-nwu kudɔ ɔnini, “Biti baƒiɔ udu biala nse ɔdi kudɔ ƒui bivaakun. Na bɛbla lɛlɔ kedee na bɛvumana bina adala.

ref: Afterwards, we'll go to Bethel. I will build an altar there for God, who answered my prayers when I was in trouble and who has always been at my side.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama na bibo na buteesie Betel kesii kani na ndubɔtu lɛkpabi lite Unwoti Katɔti. Anwu nénu-mi bɔkpali uwi ɔni na ala eekpóku-mi, na ɔ́gyumana-mi kesi kesii na lɛ́vi.”

ref: So everyone gave Jacob their idols and their earrings, and he buried them under the oak tree near Shechem.
text: Ka ɔ́su-ma lɛni ni, bálɛku baƒiɔ lidu ƒui béboku-nwu na Yakob úku-ma ɔ́kya kugyii bonwu kɔdi nnye mmɔ kalɔ na Sekem mmɔ.

ref: While Jacob and his family were traveling through Canaan, God terrified the people in the towns so much that no one dared bother them.
text: Nwuna kama ni, báyu ulu na bátɔlɔ. Na Unwoti Katɔti ɔyikun ɔni na kadi kammɔ bekyuli bánu ɔsu ni, liƒu lɛ́da-ma na Yakob ku anwu badi lɛlɔ ɔsu ni, bátakana na basia-ma kama.

ref: Finally, they reached Bethel, also known as Luz.
text: Yakob ku anwu badi bébedu Betel kesii kani basɔ baalɔ banii Lus.

ref: Jacob built an altar there and called it “God of Bethel,” because that was the place where God had appeared to him when he was running from Esau.
text: Mmɔ na ɔ́tu lɛkpabi na ɔ́sia-ni lɛnyi ɔnii El Betel, ɔsika mmɔ na Unwoti Katɔti údie lɛlɔ ɔ́tina-nwu uwi ɔni na uukeli ɔnwani Esau.

ref: While they were there, Rebekah's personal servant Deborah died. They buried her under an oak tree and called it “Weeping Oak.” God Blesses Jacob at Bethel
text: Na uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo Yakob ulekudi Rebeka ubo ulekubi ɔni nátaanu-nwu kaduli lɛlɔ. Ulekubi ɔmɛni baalɔ Debora, úbekpi na báva-nwu na kugyii bonwu kɔmmɔ kani kalɔ si ási Betel áyu ésela kada kakɛ. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu baalɔ kugyii kammɔ Keku Kugyii iye Alon Bakut na bana butemi kemmo.

ref: I will give you the land that I promised Abraham and Isaac, and it will belong to your family forever.
text: Na kalɔ kani na líti líte Abraham ku Isak ni, aka kani na líti muute-ɔ. Na kalɔ kamɛni na ndubuti lite ƒula kaba kemmo bebi.”

ref: Jacob set up a large rock, so that he would remember what had happened there. Then he poured wine and olive oil on the rock to show that it was dedicated to God,
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Yakob úti lɛtabi lɛdi únyene aani lɛkpa lɛdi ute Unwoti Katɔti na kesii kammɔ kalɛ na utaakali lɛla lɛni nálɛ mmɔ. Na úti ntɛ bɔdi úwi lɛtabi lɛmmɔ lɛlɔ. Lɛni kani na úti nnɔmii bɔdi úwi-ni lɛlɔ.

ref: and he named the place Bethel. Benjamin Is Born
text: Na kesii kani na Unwoti Katɔti únye ɔ́tɛmiku-nwu mmɔ ni, Yakob ɔ́sia lɛnyi ɔnii Betel. Báwɔ Benyamin

ref: Jacob and his family had left Bethel and were still a long way from Ephrath, when the time came for Rachel's baby to be born.
text: Na mmɔ bulie kama ni, básɔ báta bésela Eƒrata. Na ulu lɛlɔ ni, kawɔƒɔ kébo kaadu Rahel na keekpóku-nwu geen. Na liluku geen lɛnɔ nébu asa na beduo Eƒrata.

ref: She was having a rough time, but the woman who was helping her said, “Don't worry! It's a boy.”
text: Ka kɔwɔ kɔmmɔ kɔdaku-nwu bude ɔsu ni, uleku ɔni mɔɔƒɔ́-nwu kɔwɔ ɔ́kya-nwu ɔkplu. Na ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Sɔ tayia. Ubi ɔnanabi na ɔdi.”

ref: Rachel was at the point of death, and right before dying, she said, “I'll name him Benoni.” But Jacob called him Benjamin.
text: Na ka ɔdaku bɔyula ni, Rahel ɔ́sia ubi ɔmmɔ lɛnyi ɔnii Ben-Oni. Nsu ubi ɔti anwu ɔ́lɔ-nwu ɔnii Benyamin, na nwuna kalɔ ndi ubi ɔni na naaaƒianku.

ref: Rachel was buried beside the road to Ephrath, which is also called Bethlehem.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Rahel úkpi na báva-nwu na Eƒrata ulu lɛlɔ mmɔ kesii kani baalɔ Betlehem.

ref: Jacob set up a tombstone over her grave, and it is still there.
text: Yakob úti lɛtabi lɛdi ɔ́lɛ kaƒiɔtɔ kammɔ lɛlɔ aani lɛkpa. Na úbalɛ ɔmwi limeyi lɛmɛni lɛlɔ ni, ɔdi likposela lɛni mɔɔtina Rahel Kaƒiɔtɔ.

ref: Jacob, also known as Israel, traveled to the south of Eder Tower, where he set up camp.
text: Amu ka bésie anumi blii ni, Yakob ɔni lɛnɔ na basɔ baalɔ Israel ni, ɔ́sɔ ɔ́tu nvo bɔdi na kesii kani baalɔ Migdal Eder kama kakɛ blii.

ref: During their time there, Jacob's oldest son Reuben slept with Bilhah, who was one of Jacob's other wives. And Jacob found out about it. Jacob's Twelve Sons
text: Uwi ɔni na Israel ku anwu badi bakya kalɔ kammɔ kemmo ni, ubidi Ruben ku ɔti ulekudi Bilha bátɛ na ɔti úbenu lɛla lɛmɛni. Bebi leevu-inyɔ na Yakob ɔ́wɔ.

ref: Jacob went to his father Isaac at Hebron, also called Mamre or Kiriath-Arba, where Isaac's father Abraham had lived as a foreigner.
text: Na Yakob ulu busie kemmo ni, úbedu ɔti kudɔ na bana ɔma Mamre ɔni na ude Kiriat Arba ɔni basɔ baalɔ banii Hebron. Mmɔ na Abraham ku Isak bélie dii.

ref: Esau, also known as Edom, had many descendants.
text: Esau ɔni basɔ baalɔ lɛnɔ banii Edom lɛlɔ kɔtɛ na bɔdubo bunu mɛni.

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Esau úti beleku ɛtɛ bani nási Kanaan. Udeedi ndi Ada ɔni ɔti ndi Hitini ɔni baalɔ Elon. Na uleku unyɔdi na baalɔ Oholibama. Nwuna ɔti ndi Ana, na nwuna nana ɔnana ndi Sibeon ɔni ndi Hewini.

ref: the third was Basemath, who was Ishmael's daughter and Nebaioth's sister.
text: Na uleku ɔtɛdi ndi Basmat. Na ɔti ndi Ismael na ɔnwani ulekubi ndi Nebaiot.

ref: Esau and his three wives had five sons while in Canaan. Adah's son was Eliphaz; Basemath's son was Reuel; Oholibamah's three sons were Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text: Na Oholibama ɔ́wɔ Yeus ku Yalam ku Kora úte-nwu. Bebi bammɔ na báwɔ béte Esau na Kanaan.

ref: Esau took his children and wives, his relatives and servants, his animals and possessions he had acquired while in Canaan, and moved far from Jacob.
text: Esau úti nwuna beleku ku bebi bananabi ku belekubi ku nwuna kaduli bekyuli ƒui ɔ́tɔlɔku kesii kani na ɔ́kya mmɔ. Lɛni kani na úti nwuna bebuo ƒui ku nwuna akɛla ani ƒui na ɔ́bla na Kanaan mmɔ na ɔ́tɔlɔ úsie kalɔ bamba lɛlɔ na ɔ́gyunku ɔnwani Yakob ƒui.

ref: He did this because the land was too crowded and could not support him and his brother with their flocks and herds.
text: Bana inyɔ ala ani bebo emomo geen ɔsu ni, balakana likuku linwi bulie. Na kalɔ kani lɛlɔ na bakya mmɔ keledu bana bebuo akpa anyɔ ammɔ bɔdi.

ref: That's why Esau made his home in the hill country of Seir.
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu Esau, ɔni basɔ baalɔ Edom, ɔ́tɔlɔ úsie abi kalɔ kani baalɔ Seir lɛlɔ.

ref: Esau and Adah's oldest son was Eliphaz, and the clans that descended from him were Teman, Omar, Zepho, Kenaz,
text: Ɔƒɛni ni, baga badi bákya Esau kaba bebi akɔ. Na Esau ubidi udeedi ɔni ndi Eliƒas bebi bananabi akɔ ni, baga bammɔ ndi Teman ku Omar ku Seƒo ku Kenas

ref: Korah, Gatam, and Amalek. These and Esau's other descendants lived in the land of Edom.
text: ku Kora ku Gatam ku Amalek. Eliƒas kaba kemmo baga na uwi ɔni bákya Edom kalɔ lɛlɔ na badi mmɔ. Na badi Esau ulekudi Ada bawɔ.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah were Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text: Na Esau ulekudi Oholibama bebi bananabi akɔ ni, baga bani nákya ndi Yeus ku Yalam ku Kora. Ama lɛnɔ nálɛ bási Oholibama ɔni ndi Ana ubidi bebi akɔ.

ref: All of these clans descended from Esau, who was known as Edom.
text: Bekyuli bani anyi na búnu mmɔ ndi baga bani nálɛ bási Esau, ɔni baalɔ lɛnɔ banii Edom, kaba bebi akɔ.

ref: Seir was from the Horite tribe that had lived in Edom before the time of Esau. The clans that had descended from him were Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
text: Na Seir ɔni ndi Horini ku nwuna bebi bananabi bani nélie kalɛkɛ kammɔ lɛlɔ na bádi lɛga ndi Lotan ku Sobal ku Sibeon ku Ana

ref: Lotan's sons were Hori and Heman; his sister was Timna.
text: Lotan bebi bananabi ndi Hori ku Hemam. Na Lotan ɔnwani ulekubi ndi Timna.

ref: Shobal's sons were Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
text: Na Sobal bebi bananabi bani na ɔ́wɔ ndi Alwan ku Manahat ku Ebal ku Seƒo ku Onam.

ref: Zibeon's sons were Aiah and Anah—the same Anah who found an oasis in the desert while taking the donkeys of his father out to pasture.
text: Na Sibeon bebi bananabi ndi Aya ku Ana. Ana ɔmɛni nésie útánu abiɔ ani ntu ndi utugya ka ɔ́kpanku ɔti Sibeon bebuo ala betine úsienku kadikɔ.

ref: Dishon's sons were Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
text: Na Dison bebi bananabi ndi Hendan ku Esban ku Itran ku Keran.

ref: Ezer's sons were Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.
text: Eser bebi bananabi ndi Bilhan ku Saawan ku Akan.

ref: Dishan's sons were Uz and Aran.
text: Disan bebi bananabi ndi Us ku Aran.

ref: Before there were kings in Israel, the following kings ruled Edom one after another: Bela son of Beor from Dinhabah; Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah; Husham from the land of Teman; Hadad son of Bedad from Avith ; Samlah from Masrekah; Shaul from the city of Rehoboth on the Euphrates River; Baalhanan son of Achbor; Hadar from the city of Pau .
text: Na nwuna kama ni, Hadar nébelie-nwu kenye. Uwi ɔni na Baal-Hanan úkpi ni, Hadad nélie-nwu kenye aani ɔga. Na ɔma ɔni na úlie baalɔ Pau. Na ulekudi ndi Matred ubidi ulekubi Mehetabel na ɔdi Me-Sahab uwɔ.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau took their names from their families and the places where they lived. They are Timna, Alvah, Jetheth,
text: Baga badi bási Esau kaba bebi akɔ na bana adua ku kɔlɛkɛ kɔni lɛlɔ na bádi kenye, na bana anyi ndi Timna ku Ahoa ku Yetet

ref: Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar,
text: Kenas ku Teman ku Mibsar ku

ref: Magdiel, and Iram. These clans descended from Esau, who was known as Edom, the father of the Edomites. They took their names from the places where they settled.
text: Magdiel ku Iram. Bekyuli bamɛni nádi lɛga na Edom kalɔ lɛlɔ na kalɛ na báda bana lɛma na kalɔ kani kemmo na bákya kemmo. Na Esau ndi Edomƒuɔ ɔti.

ref: Jacob lived in the land of Canaan, where his father Isaac had lived,
text: Kanaan kalɔ kani kemmo na Yakob ɔti ɔ́kya ni, mmɔ kani na Yakob lɛnɔ úlie.

ref: “They're not here anymore,” the man replied. “I overheard them say they were going to Dothan.” Joseph left and found his brothers in Dothan.
text: Mmɔ na ɔnana ɔmmɔ ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Báatɔlɔ mɛni. Na línu lɛnɔ ka baasu banini, ‘Bɛya na buteesie Dotan kalɔ kemmo.’ ” Ɔmmɔ ɔ́su ni, Yoseƒ ɔ́sia nwuna banwani kama na útánu-ma na Dotan kudɔ mmɔ.

ref: Jacob loved Joseph more than he did any of his other sons, because Joseph was born when Jacob was very old. Jacob had even given Joseph a fancy coat
text: Ɔƒɛni ni, Yoseƒ ɔti Israel ni, ɔ́ɔbi lɛlɔ geen asa na ɔ́wɔ Yoseƒ ɔsu ni, ɔɔyɔ́ Yoseƒ geen. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ni, ɔ́ya na bédi awu gyɔlɔɔ ɔdi ɔni mɔɔdia bɔnu geen béte Yoseƒ.

ref: which showed that Joseph was his favorite son, and so Joseph's brothers hated him and would not be friendly to him.
text: Ka nwuna banwani bánu báti ka bana ɔti ɔɔyɔ́ Yoseƒ teetulo-ma ɔsu ni, balátɛmiku-nwu ku lɛdi buɛɛ munu munu. Na beekisi-nwu.

ref: One day, Joseph told his brothers what he had dreamed, and they hated him even more.
text: Yoseƒ úgye kɔna kɔdi. Na ka ɔ́su nwuna banwani kɔna kɔmɛni ni, ɔ́ya na basɔ beekisi-nwu teebu.

ref: Joseph said, “Let me tell you about my dream.
text: Limeyi lɛdi ni, ɔ́su nwuna banwani ɔnini, “Bise atu na binu kɔna kɔni na lígye.

ref: We were out in the field, tying up bundles of wheat. Suddenly my bundle stood up, and your bundles gathered around and bowed down to it.”
text: Lígye kɔna ka bunye bɔɔnwa kamu teenyimi esu esu teewi. Kɔnakunwi ni, mina lisu lɛ́ta línye ligeen. Na amii adi ébo ábaka ámana ami ɔdi. Na áyɔlɔ eli ákya ami ɔdi kalɔ.”

ref: His brothers asked, “Do you really think you are going to be king and rule over us?” Now they hated Joseph more than ever because of what he had said about his dream.
text: Mmɔ ni, nwuna banwani béye-nwu banini, “Bɔkali na ada anii awɔ ndubo-bu lɛlɔ butaadi?” Ɔmɛni ɔsu bukisi bɔmmɔ bɔ́kɔ katɔ búbu na kɔna kɔni na úti ɔ́su-ma ɔsu.

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: Na ɔmmɔ kama ni, ɔ́sɔ úgye kɔna kɔdi na ɔ́sɔ ɔ́su nwuna banwani ɔnini, “Bɛsɔ binu kɔna bamba kɔni na lígye. Lɛ́nu ka ɔƒɛ ku kɔbuli ku ayabi leevu-unwi eeboku lɛlɔ kalɔ teete-mi.”

ref: When he told his father about this dream, his father became angry and said, “What's that supposed to mean? Are your mother and I and your brothers all going to come and bow down to you?”
text: Ka útásu ɔti lɛnɔ kɔna kɔni na úgye na nwuna banwani anumi ni, ɔti ɔ́dɔnku-nwu na ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Bi kɔna na égye mɛni. Litemi geen na lɛdi mɛni ka ƒ'ini ku ƒula banwani ku ami ɔlati bɔdubo lili kalɔ bɔkya bute-ɔ?”

ref: Joseph's brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept wondering about the dream. Joseph Is Sold and Taken to Egypt
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu nwuna lɛlɔ líbo liigyie nwuna banwani anumi, nsu ɔti úti litemi lɛmɛni ɔ́kya kasusu kemmo. Yoseƒ Banwani Béti Yoseƒ Béye

ref: One day when Joseph's brothers had taken the sheep to a pasture near Shechem,
text: Na uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo ni, Yoseƒ banwani bákpanku bana ɔti bebuo bésienku kadikɔ na alakpi akɔ na Sekem kudɔ.

ref: his father Jacob said to him, “I want you to go to your brothers. They are with the sheep near Shechem.” “Yes, sir,” Joseph answered.
text: Na Israel ɔ́su Yoseƒ ɔnini, “Agyi kesii kani na ƒula banwani bákpanku bebuo bésienku kadikɔ na alakpi akɔ na Sekem kudɔ. Bo, na lɛtala-a bana kudɔ.” Amu na údie kenye ɔnini, “Ati, ngyi mmɔ kaduɛ.”

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: Mmɔ na ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Sie na atánu si ƒula banwani ku bebuo bammɔ ƒui bakya kaduɛ na lɛla lɛdi lítabla ɔdi. Na asɔ enwini ebo abasu-mi.” Amu na údie-nwu ulu ɔ́si Hebron Ɔkwalɛ mmɔ. Ka Yoseƒ údu Sekem mmɔ ni, ɔkya ɔɔka taamana alakpi akɔ mmɔ,

ref: and wandering through the fields, when a man asked, “What are you looking for?”
text: na ukyuli ɔdi úbanu-nwu. Mmɔ na ukyuli ɔmmɔ úye-nwu ɔnini, “Bi na aawia?”

ref: Joseph answered, “I'm looking for my brothers who are watching the sheep. Can you tell me where they are?”
text: Yoseƒ údie kenye ɔnini, “Mina banwani na mɔɔwia. Buvele, ánu kesii kani na béti bebuo bésienku kadikɔ?”

ref: But before he got there, they saw him coming and made plans to kill him.
text: Nsu ama báka-nwu bɔnu ka ɔkɛ ɔgyu ɔdubo. Na asa na webedu-ma kudɔ ni, báasa lili ka badu-nwu.

ref: They said to one another, “Look, here comes the hero of those dreams!
text: Ka ɔ́ni úde-ma ni, básu lɛlɔ banini, “Bɛɛnu ukyuli ɔni muugyé ana mmɔ.”

ref: Reuben heard this and tried to protect Joseph from them. “Let's not kill him,” he said.
text: Ka Ruben únu kenye kamɛni ni, ɔ́sia ulu biala lɛlɔ ka údie-nwu ngba na bana enu kemmo. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ɔ́su-ma ɔnini, “Bitaya na bubile-nwu ngba.

ref: “Don't murder him or even harm him. Just throw him into a well out here in the desert.” Reuben planned to rescue Joseph later and take him back to his father.
text: Na bitaya na buwi ubugya kalɔ. Bibo-nwu bɛkya abiɔ amɛni ɔdi kemmo na ketele mɛni. Nsu bitetuku-nwu ulu bamba ɔdi lɛlɔ munu munu.” Ruben kasusu ndi ka ɔɔsu-ma lɛni na kama na udie-nwu ɔsɔku usienku bana ɔti.

ref: When Joseph came to his brothers, they pulled off his fancy coat
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ka Yoseƒ úbedu-ma kudɔ ni, ɔkya nwuna awu gyɔlɔɔ ɔni náƒɔ kuye. Amu na bédie-nwu awu ɔmmɔ.

ref: and threw him into a dry well.
text: Na béti-nwu bébo bákya libiɔ lɛni kemmo na ntu buuuwi mmɔ kemmo.

ref: As Joseph's brothers sat down to eat, they looked up and saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead. Their camels were loaded with all kinds of spices that they were taking to Egypt.
text: Uwi ɔni na bélie kalɔ banii baadi ni, bánu ka bebuo ala betine beti ala pii adi badubo. Na badi Ismael kadi bekyuli bani nási Gilead. Bebuo bamɛni béti kutu akyala lidu pii ku ala awɛɛdi ƒɛƒɛɛ bésela Egyipte kalɔ lɛlɔ.

ref: So Judah said, “What will we gain if we kill our brother and hide his body?
text: Mmɔ ni, Yuda ɔ́su nwuna banwani ɔnini, “Bi na bɔdubo bɔkɛ si bɔ́du bula ɔnwani na búti ala búbi nwuna ubugya?

ref: Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites and not harm him. After all, he is our brother.” And the others agreed.
text: Bɛya na buti-nwu buye bute Ismael kadi bekyuli bamɛni na abu buleti bula enu bulabla-nwu lɛla lɛdi. Kavumana kadi keeebo abu ku-nwu ntɛ, bula ɔnwani kani na ɔdi.” Amu na nwuna banwani bammɔ lɛnɔ bénu kalɔ.

ref: When Reuben returned to the well and did not find Joseph there, he tore his clothes in sorrow.
text: Ka Ruben únwini úsie libiɔ lɛmmɔ kɔtu na ɔ́nu ka Yoseƒ ɔsɔ uuubo ubu ɔmmɔ kemmo ni, ɔdi lɛla lɛni nékpoku-nwu geen ɔsu ɔ́ni awu ɔni na ɔkya ɔ́bwɛ kalɛ na ama baabla.

ref: Then he went back to his brothers and said, “The boy is gone! What am I going to do?”
text: Na úsie nwuna banwani kudɔ útasu-ma ɔnini, “Ɔnamu ɔmmɔ ɔsɔ uuubo mmɔ! Dɛ mabla?”

ref: Joseph's brothers killed a goat and dipped Joseph's fancy coat in its blood.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, nwuna banwani bammɔ bádu ese ɔdi. Na béti Yoseƒ awu ɔmmɔ bákya ubugya ɔmmɔ kemmo.

ref: After this, they took the coat to their father and said, “We found this! Look at it carefully and see if it belongs to your son.”
text: Béti awu ɔbuni ɔmmɔ bésienku bana ɔti na bátásu-nwu banini, “Awu ɔmɛni na bɔ́kɛ ka bɔ́si bɔdubo. Sia-nwu anumi kaduɛ na anu si ƒ'ubidi awu na ɔdi.”

ref: Jacob knew it was Joseph's coat and said, “It's my son's coat! Joseph has been torn to pieces and eaten by some wild animal.”
text: Amu ka bana ɔti ɔ́nu awu ɔmmɔ ni, ɔ́su ɔnini, “Mina ubi awu na ɔdi! Eƒiebuo ɔdi nádu Yoseƒ na ɔ́duli-nwu ɔ́kun lɛni!”

ref: Jacob mourned for Joseph a long time, and to show his sorrow he tore his clothes and wore sackcloth.
text: Na litemi lɛmmɔ líkpoku-nwu geen, anwuɔsu ɔ́ni nwuna awu ɔ́bwɛ. Na úti adikpi únyimi na úwulo ubidi emeyi pii.

ref: All of Jacob's children came to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. “No,” he said, “I will go to my grave, mourning for my son.” So Jacob kept on grieving.
text: Yakob bebi bananabi ku belekubi ƒui bébo bábasia-nwu kɔnu, nsu útada buwulo útaya. Na ɔ́su ɔnini, “Lɛla lɛmɛni lilása-mi buduo. Na ndubo buwulo laa ntéduo kaƒiɔtɔ kemmo na mina ubi kudɔ.” Ɔmmɔ ɔsu úwulo ubidi ɔmmɔ geen.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Na uwi ɔni kemmo na Yakob ɔɔbla ɔmɛni ƒui ni, Midianƒuɔ bamɛni béeti Yoseƒ béye bete Egyipteni ɔni baalɔ Potiƒar na ɔdi Ɔga Ƒarao benengu akɔ unwi ɔni mɔɔnu bekyuli bani mɔɔgyumana ɔga unengudi lɛlɔ.

ref: About that time Judah left his brothers in the hill country and went to live near his friend Hirah in the town of Adullam.
text: Na uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo ni, Yuda ɔ́tɔlɔ nwuna banwani kudɔ úsie ɔnana ɔdi baalɔ banii Hira kudɔ na Adulam ɔma kemmo.

ref: While there he met the daughter of Shua, a Canaanite man. Judah married her,
text: Mmɔ na Yuda útánu Kanaanni ɔnana ɔdi baalɔ banii Sua ubidi ulekubi. Yuda úti uleku ɔmɛni na úsie-nwu kudɔ.

ref: and they had three sons. He named the first one Er;
text: Ulekubi ɔmɛni úbada kaƒɔ na ɔ́wɔ ɔnanabi. Na ɔ́sia-nwu lɛnyi ɔnii Er.

ref: she named the next one Onan.
text: Ɔ́sɔ úbada kaƒɔ ɔmmɔ kama na ɔ́wɔ ɔnanabi bamba ɔni baalɔ Onan.

ref: The third one was born when Judah was in Chezib, and she named him Shelah.
text: Kɔtɛdi ka ɔ́sɔ ɔwɔ ni, ɔ́wɔ ɔnanabi. Na baalɔ anwu banii Sela. Na ubi ɔmɛni ni, ɔma ɔni baalɔ banii Kesib kemmo na útáwɔ-nwu.

ref: Later, Judah chose Tamar as a wife for Er, his oldest son.
text: Na uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo ni, Yuda úti uleku úte ubidi udeedi ɔni ndi Er. Na ulekudi ɔmmɔ baalɔ Tamar.

ref: But Er was very evil, and the Lord took his life.
text: Nsu Er ablala na Unwoti Katɔti anumi ni, ɔɔtina ka ubo kalala. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ni, Unwoti Katɔti ɔ́ya na Er úkpi.

ref: Onan knew the child would not be his, and when he had sex with Tamar, he made sure that she would not get pregnant.
text: Ɔƒɛni ni, Onan ɔgyi ka si anwu ku ɔnwani ulekudi ɔmmɔ báwɔ ni, bebi bammɔ baaadi anwu ɔlati badi. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu si baatɛ na lɛlɔ ntu bɔdubo-nwu ni, na ɔ́ta uleku ɔmmɔ lɛlɔ na ntu bɔmmɔ buwi kalɔ kalɛ na uleku ɔmmɔ ulada kaƒɔ na ɔwɔ ute ɔnwani.

ref: The Lord wasn't pleased with Onan and took his life too.
text: Na Katɔti ɔ́nu ka lɛla lɛni na Onan lɛnɔ ɔɔbla mɛni lɛdi kalala na nwuna anumi ɔsu ɔ́ya na anwu lɛnɔ úkpi.

ref: Judah did not want the same thing to happen to his son Shelah, and he told Tamar, “Go home to your father and live there as a widow until my son Shelah is grown.” So Tamar went to live with her father.
text: Ala amɛni bɔlɛ lɛni kama ni, Yuda ɔ́su Tamar ubidi ukpinele ɔmɛni ɔnini, “Teesie na etélie ƒ'ɔti kaduli aani ukpinele wetédu ka mina ubidi ukplodi Sela ɔ́bi lɛlɔ.” Nsu geen ni, Yuda ɔ́su lɛlɔ ɔnini, “Ɔƒɛni kalɛ Sela lɛnɔ wekpi aani nwuna banwani kalɛ.” Anwuɔsu Tama lɛnɔ ɔ́tɔlɔ úsie ɔti kaduli.

ref: Tamar found out that her father-in-law Judah was going to Timnah to shear his sheep.
text: Uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo ni, ukyuli ɔdi úbo na ɔ́su Tamar ɔnini, “Ƒula ɔgyanti úti ala úsela Timnat na betékulo nwuna baƒonu linwini.”

ref: She also realized that Shelah was now a grown man, but she had not been allowed to marry him. So she decided to dress in something other than her widow's clothes and to cover her face with a veil. After this, she sat outside the town of Enaim on the road to Timnah.
text: Amu na ɔ́babla na ɔ́ta útédie likpinele adala úse. Na úti lɛduni lɛdi úbi lili ku anumi na ɔ́vumana lɛlɔ. Na ɔ́ta úsie uli Enaim ɔma kabanye na ulu ɔni néti úsela Timnat lɛlɔ. Tamar ukpinele ɔmɛni ɔ́nu ɔ́ti ka ina ka Sela ɔ́ɔbi lɛlɔ na ɔkana na uti-nwu na uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo ni, nwuna ɔgyanti Yuda útabasu-nwu tɔ lɛla lɛdi úti úbedu uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo na útélie Sela kudɔ aani ulekudi.

ref: When Judah came along, he did not recognize her because of the veil. He thought she was a prostitute
text: Amu ka Yuda úbedu mmɔ na ɔ́nu-nwu ni, ɔ́bu ɔnii ɔyalɛ ɔdi na ɔdi, ɔsika úti lɛduni úbi anumi.

ref: “One of my young goats,” he answered. “What will you give me to keep until you send the goat?” she asked.
text: Mmɔ na Yuda údie kenye ɔnini, “Ndubuti ese ɔkɔdi ɔdi limene-ɔ lɛsiku mina bebuo akɔ.” Amu na uleku ɔmmɔ lɛnɔ ɔ́sɔ úye-nwu ɔnini, “Adubuti lɛla lɛdi enyene lɛlɔ kama eti atina ka adubɔdi ƒula litemi lɛni na aasu mɛni lɛlɔ?”

ref: “What do you want?” he asked in return. “The ring on that cord around your neck,” was her reply. “I also want the special walking stick you have with you.” He gave them to her, they slept together, and she became pregnant.
text: Mmɔ na Yuda úye-nwu ɔnini, “Bi na aayɔ́ ka liti linyene lɛlɔ kama?” Amu na uleku ɔmmɔ ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Lɛla lɛni na eeti teese ƒula kunweni ku ala bamba likpo ku kabuli kani nda-nwu lɛlɔ ku ƒula ugyii ɔdadi.” Ɔmmɔ ɔsu Yuda lɛnɔ únu kalɔ na úti ala ammɔ úte-nwu na anwu ku-nwu bátɛ na úbada kaƒɔ lɛnɔ.

ref: After returning home, Tamar took off the veil and dressed in her widow's clothes again.
text: Na ka Yuda ɔ́tɔlɔ ni, ubidi ule ɔmɛni ɔ́sɔ útávumana adala na úti nwuna ukpinele ala ammɔ kani ɔ́kya.

ref: Judah asked his friend Hirah take a goat to the woman, so he could get back the ring and walking stick, but she wasn't there.
text: Na kalɛ na básu bése ɔsu ni, Yuda úti ese ɔkɔdi ɔmmɔ úte nwuna ɔkpana Adulamni ɔmmɔ ɔnii uti uboku ɔyalɛ ɔmmɔ na ɔƒɔ ala ani na úti únyene lɛlɔ kama mmɔ uboku-nwu, nsu nwuna ɔkpana ɔmmɔ útanu-nwu.

ref: Hirah asked the people of Enaim, “Where is the prostitute who sat along the road outside your town?” “There's never been one here,” they answered.
text: Anwuɔsu na úye banana bani nli amadɔ mmɔ ɔnini, “Wɛ na ɔyalɛ ɔni na búbanu na Enaim ulu lɛlɔ na kabanye mɛni ɔ́vi?” Amu na bédie kenye banini, “Ɔyalɛ ɔdi ulelie mɛni.”

ref: Hirah went back and told Judah, “I couldn't find the woman, and the people of Enaim said no prostitute had ever been there.”
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ɔ́sɔ únwini úsie na útásu Yuda ɔnini, “Ńtanu ɔyalɛ ɔmmɔ. Na lɛnɔ ni, banana bani nkya amadɔ mmɔ básu-mi ka ɔyalɛ ɔdi lɛnɔ útakya mɛni kudii.”

ref: “If you couldn't find her, we'll just let her keep the things I gave her,” Judah answered. “And we'd better forget about the goat, or else we'll look like fools.”
text: Mmɔ na Yuda ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Ya na uleku ɔmmɔ uti ala ammɔ ƒaa. Si ɔɔɔdi lɛni ni, bɔdubo lɛmala bunwini na bekyuli anumi. Na ami lɛ́nu ka lɛ́bla lɛ́siaku bula katɛmise lɛlɔ nka. Na bɔnu na átanu-nwu.”

ref: About three months later someone told Judah, “Your daughter-in-law Tamar has behaved like a prostitute, and now she's pregnant!” “Drag her out of town and burn her to death!” Judah shouted.
text: Ɔbla aani abuli atɛ kama ni, bekyuli badi bábasu Yuda banini, “Bɔ́nu ka ƒ'ubidi ule Tamar ɔ́ƒiɔ, ɔsika úti lɛlɔ ɔ́bla ɔyalɛ. Na ɔmmɔ ɔsu ɔda kaƒɔ ɔƒɛni.” Mmɔ na Yuda ɔ́su-ma ɔnini, “Bɛlɛku-nwu biboku na bɛkyula-nwu na ukpi.”

ref: As Tamar was being dragged off, she sent someone to tell her father-in-law, “The man who gave me this ring, this cord, and this walking stick is the one who got me pregnant.”
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ka bététiku-nwu badububoku ni, ɔ́tala ukyuli ɔ́kya nwuna ɔƒanti Yuda ɔnini, “Ukyuli ɔni mbo ala ani na nda mɛni nákya-mi kaƒɔ. Anwuɔsu mɔɔwia ka asia-nya anu kaduɛ anu si adubɔnu ati ukyuli ɔni mbo lɛla lɛni na beesé kunweni ku ala bamba likpo mɛni ku kabuli kani nda-nwu lɛku ku ugyii ɔdadi ɔmɛni lɛlɔ.”

ref: “Those are mine!” Judah admitted. “She's a better person than I am, because I broke my promise to let her marry my son Shelah.” After this, Judah never slept with her again.
text: Yuda ɔ́nu ala ammɔ ɔ́ti na ɔ́su ɔnini, “Uleku ɔmɛni use siin utulo ami ɔlati, ɔsika ami néteti-nwu ńtete mina ubi Sela na uti.” Na ɔmmɔ kama ni, anwu ku uleku ɔmmɔ básɔ bétesie lɛlɔ kudɔ.

ref: Tamar later gave birth to twins. But before either of them was born, one of them stuck a hand out of her womb. The woman who was helping tied a red thread around the baby's hand and explained, “This one came out first.”
text: Na kawɔlɔkɔ ni, ubi unwi údie kɔnu ɔ́lɛku. Na uleku ɔni mɔɔƒɔ́ kɔwɔ mɛni úti unwo kabuli kegyiedi kadi úbo ɔ́da-nwu kɔnu na ɔ́su ɔnini, “Ubi ɔmɛni náka bɔlɛ.”

ref: At once his hand went back in, and the other child was born first. The woman then said, “What an opening you've made for yourself!” So they named the baby Perez.
text: Nsu ka ubi ɔmmɔ ɔ́sɔku kɔnu ɔ́kya uni lɛlɔ kemmo ni, ubi unyɔdi mmomu náka bɔlɛ ka ɔ́wɔ-ma. Anwuɔsu na kɔwɔ ɔƒɔne ɔmmɔ ɔ́su ɔnini, “Ɔmɛni anwu bɔnwanɛ na ánwanɛ lɛlɔ álɛ.” Na básia-nwu lɛnyi banii Peres.

ref: When the brother with the red thread was born, they named him Zerah.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama asa na ɔnwani ɔni kɔnu na lɛduni ligyiedi lɛda úbalɛ. Na básia anwu lɛnyi banii Sera.

ref: The Ishmaelites took Joseph to Egypt and sold him to Potiphar, the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Na uwi ɔmmɔ kemmo ni, báakpanku Yoseƒ béboku Egyipte na béye-nwu béte unengu ɔni baalɔ Potiƒar. Na Potiƒar ɔmɛni ɔdi Ɔga Ƒarao benengu akɔ unwi. Na anwu ndi bani ƒui mɔɔgyumana ɔga ɔmmɔ unengu.

ref: So Joseph lived in the home of Potiphar, his Egyptian owner. Soon Potiphar realized that the Lord was helping Joseph to be successful in whatever he did.
text: Ka nwuna ɔtɔlati ɔ́nu ɔ́ti kalɛ na Unwoti Katɔti uliku Yoseƒ ku kalɛ na nwuna ablala aatɛ-nwu ɔsu ni,

ref: Potiphar liked Joseph and made him his personal assistant, putting him in charge of his house and all of his property.
text: Yoseƒ lɛlɔ lɛ́da-nwu kubiti geen. Anwuɔsu úti Yoseƒ únwini ukyuli ɔni na ɔɔtala na ɔɔbla nwuna kaduli ala teete-nwu ɔni na Potiƒar lɛla biala lɛkya-nwu enu kemmo.

ref: Potiphar left everything up to Joseph, and with Joseph there, the only decision he had to make was what he wanted to eat. Joseph was well-built and handsome,
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu geen na Potiƒar ɔ́nyonu lɛla biala ɔ́kya Yoseƒ enu kemmo. Na údie lɛla biala atu na lɛla lɛdi lɛsɔ lɛɛɛkɛ-nwu ntu adila ani kani na badubuti beboku-nwu na ɔdi kani. Ɔƒɛni Yoseƒ ɔmɛni ni, ɔ́lɛ lɛkloƒu na ɔɔdia bɔnu geen.

ref: and Potiphar's wife soon noticed him. She asked him to make love to her,
text: Yoseƒ ɔtɔlati ulekudi ɔkya ɔɔsia okloƒu ɔmɛni anu, ɔsika nwuna ala áda-nwu kayi. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ɔ́lɔ-nwu na ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Bo na aya na bɔtɛ!”

ref: No one in my master's house is more important than I am. The only thing he hasn't given me is you, and that's because you are his wife. I won't sin against God by doing such a terrible thing as this.”
text: Nsu Yoseƒ úbulo na ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Ɔni na mina ɔtɔlati úti-mi únyene nwuna ala ƒui kenye ni, údie lɛla biala atu. Na ukyuli ɔdi lɛnɔ uuutulo-mi na kaduli kamɛni, ɔsika mina enu kemmo na lɛla biala lɛkya. Mina ɔtɔlati útebu lɛla lɛdi útese ɔni na úteti-ni útakya-mi enu kemmo ntu awɔ kani. Ɔsika awɔ ndi-nwu ulekudi. Na bi ɔsu na ndubɔkana na lɛbla kɔƒa lɛla lɛmɛni udu na lɛbla likpidi lɛƒiɔ Unwoti Katɔti?”

ref: She kept begging Joseph day after day, but he refused to do what she wanted or even to go near her.
text: Na ina ka uleku ɔmɛni ɔɔsu Yoseƒ lɛlɔ kudɔ bɔtɛ litemi lɛmɛni limeyi ndi limeyi ni, Yoseƒ útenuku-nwu kalɔ iye ɔni ude-nwu muedi.

ref: One day, Joseph went to Potiphar's house to do his work, and none of the other servants were there.
text: Limeyi lɛdi ni, Yoseƒ úsie útátaabla nwuna kaduli kabla na ɔtɔ kemmo ɔni na kabla ɔblane bamba ɔdi uuubo mmɔ.

ref: Potiphar's wife grabbed hold of his coat and said, “Make love to me!” Joseph ran out of the house, leaving his coat there in her hands.
text: Amu na uleku ɔmɛni útéduo-nwu kudɔ mmɔ na úbo ɔ́da Yoseƒ na ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Bo na aya na bɔtɛ!” Mmɔ na Yoseƒ ɔ́nwanɛ lɛlɔ ɔ́lɛ, na nwuna awu gyɔlɔɔ ɔmmɔ ɔkya uleku ɔmmɔ enu kemmo.

ref: she called in her servants and said, “Look! This Hebrew has come just to make fools of us. He tried to rape me, but I screamed for help.
text: ɔ́lɔ nwuna kaduli kabla bablane. Mmɔ na ɔ́su-ma ɔnini, “Bɛnu ka Hebrini ɔni na bákpanku béboku mɛni úbo úbanu-bu ɔ́wɔlɔ. Úbo ɔnii ubatɛ-mi kudɔ.

ref: And when he heard me scream, he ran out of the house, leaving his coat with me.”
text: Amu na lɛ́va momoomo na úkeli ɔ́lɛ na nwuna awu nkya-mi enu kemmo mɛni.”

ref: Potiphar's wife kept Joseph's coat until her husband came home.
text: Uleku ɔmɛni ɔ́da awu ɔmmɔ ɔ́va nwuna ɔƒɛnyi útédu ka ɔgyani úbo kaduli.

ref: Then she said, “That Hebrew slave of yours tried to rape me!
text: Amu na ɔ́nwi kɔtɛ kɔmɛni ɔ́su ɔgyani ɔnini, “Hebrini kabla ɔblane ɔni na áƒɔ ábakya kaduli mmɔ ni, úbo-mi kudɔ ɔnii ubeƒie-mi ɔyɔ.

ref: But when I screamed for help, he left his coat and ran out of the house.”
text: Na ka lɛ́va momoomo nnii, ‘Bibebunku-mi o-o’ ni, anwu kani na úkeli ɔ́tɔlɔ na nwuna awu gyɔlɔɔ ɔtɛ-mi ɔƒɛnyi mɛni.”

ref: and threw Joseph in the same prison where the king's prisoners were kept. While Joseph was in prison,
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu úti Yoseƒ útákya ɔtɔ ɔni kemmo na baakya bekyuli bani mɔɔƒiɔ́ Ɔga Ƒarao. Nsu na ɔtɔ katɛnkɔ mmɔ lɛnɔ ni,

ref: the Lord helped him and was good to him. He even made the jailer like Joseph so much that
text: Unwoti Katɔti ɔsiaku Yoseƒ geen na ɔ́bla-nwu lɛƒɔndia ɔni na nwuna lɛlɔ lɛ́da ɔgyumanane unengu ɔmmɔ kubiti.

ref: he put him in charge of the other prisoners and of everything that was done in the jail.
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ni, ɔgyumanane ɔmmɔ lɛnɔ ɔ́ya na Yoseƒ únwini ɔɔnu bekyuli bani ƒui na batɛ ɔtɔ mmɔ lɛlɔ. Na ala ani ƒui na baabla mmɔ ni, anwu báya na unye kenye.

ref: The jailer did not worry about anything, because the Lord was with Joseph and made him successful in all that he did.
text: Na bédie lɛla biala atu na bányonu ana ƒui bákya Yoseƒ enu kemmo, ɔsika Unwoti Katɔti ɔsiaku Yoseƒ ɔni na lɛla biala na ɔɔbla ni, lɛɛtɛ kaduɛ.

ref: While Joseph was in prison, both the king's personal servant and his chief cook made the king angry. So he had them thrown into the same prison with Joseph.
text: Ɔmmɔ ɔ́su ɔ́ya na bákya-ma ɔtɔ na bagyumanane bammɔ ƒui unengu kesii. Na mmɔ kani na bákya Yoseƒ lɛnɔ ɔtɛ.

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.
text: Ɔgyumanane unengu ɔmɛni úti-ma ɔ́kya Yoseƒ kalɔ ka utaanu-ma lɛlɔ. Ka bákya ɔtɔ mmɔ emeyi adi kama ni,

ref: One night each of the two men had a dream, but their dreams had different meanings.
text: Ɔga Ƒarao ntɛ lɛlɔ ɔnune ku bodobodo lɛlɔ ɔnune bani na bákya ɔtɔ mɛni bégye ana adi na limeyi linwi lɛlɔ. Na ɔdi biala kɔna kɔni na úgye kɔdanku kalɔ budie bamba.

ref: The next morning, when Joseph went to see the men, he could tell they were upset,
text: Ka kadi kégye na Yoseƒ úbo ɔnii ubanu-ma ni, ɔ́nu ɔ́ti ka lɛla lɛdi liikpóku-ma.

ref: “We each had a dream last night,” they answered, “and there is no one to tell us what they mean.” Joseph replied, “Doesn't God know the meaning of dreams? Now tell me what you dreamed.”
text: Amu na bédie kenye banini, “Bula inyɔ ƒui búgye ana ani na bútakɛ ukyuli ɔni nedie-nya kalɔ ute-bu.” Mmɔ na Yoseƒ úye-ma ɔnini, “Bɛɛɛgyi ka Unwoti Katɔti muudie ala kalɔ? Bɛsu-mi bina ana ani na bígye.”

ref: I held the king's cup and squeezed the grapes into it, then I gave the cup to the king.”
text: Na ɔƒɛni ni, Ƒarao ɔɔƒɛɛ ɔni kemmo na ɔɔní ntɛ ɔkya-mi lɛkɔkɔ. Na lídie ebi ammɔ na lɛ́mana ntu bɔmmɔ líwi ɔɔƒɛɛ ɔmmɔ kemmo na líti lite-nwu.”

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three branches stand for three days,
text: Ka ɔ́wɔlɔ ni, Yoseƒ ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Kɔna kɔni na égye kalɔ ndi ka kugyii kɔmmɔ kɔgba ɛtɛ kɔni na ánu ni, kunye kúte emeyi atɛ.

ref: and in three days the king will pardon you. He will make you his personal servant again, and you will serve him his wine, just as you used to do.
text: Ɔƒɛni na emeyi atɛ kemmo ni, Ƒarao ɔdubɔtala kɔƒa ƒula anumi na ɔsɔku-ɔ unyene ƒula kabla kenye. Na adubuti ɔɔƒɛɛ akya Ƒarao kɔnu aani kalɛ kani na átaabla uwi ɔni na átaanu-nwu ntɛ lɛlɔ.

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.
text: Nsu si ala asɔ ábatɛ éte-ɔ kalɛ mɔɔsu mɛni ni, na enyimo na abla ami lɛnɔ lɛƒɔndia lɛdi. Mɔɔyɔ́ ka alɔ-mi lɛnyi buni ete Ƒarao na bada-mi bedie ɔtɔ ɔni na bákya-mi mɛni kemmo.

ref: I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews, and here in Egypt I haven't done anything to deserve being thrown in jail.
text: Bunye na bénye-mi lɛlɔ bákpanku-mi básiku bula Hebri kadi kalɔ lɛlɔ béboku mɛni. Na mɛni lɛnɔ ńtabla lɛla lɛni naya na beti-mi babakya kesii kani na ntɛ mɛni.”

ref: When the chief cook saw that Joseph had given a good meaning to the dream, he told Joseph, “I also had a dream. In it I was carrying three breadbaskets stacked on top of my head.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ka bodobodo uƒine ɔmmɔ únu kalɛ na Yoseƒ údie ɔnwani kɔna kalɔ na kɔdanku ala abuni ni, anwu lɛnɔ ɔ́su Yoseƒ ɔnini, “Ami lɛnɔ lígye kɔna ka nti kɔgyigyɛ ɛtɛ.

ref: The top basket was full of all kinds of baked things for the king, but birds were eating them.”
text: Na kuna kemmo ni, bodobodo ɔni béƒi bákya kɔgyigyɛ kemmo béselaku Ƒarao ɔkya, nsu baasɛ bádi-nya bédie kɔgyigyɛ kɔmmɔ kemmo na mina lili.”

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three baskets are three days,
text: Amu na Yoseƒ ɔ́su-nwu ɔnini, “Kɔna kɔni na égye ni, aku lɛnɔ kɔɔtina ka kɔgyigyɛ ɛtɛ kɔmmɔ kunye kute emeyi atɛ.

ref: and in three days the king will cut off your head. He will hang your body on a pole, and birds will come and peck at it.
text: Na emeyi atɛ ammɔ kemmo ni, Ƒarao ɔdubɔya na badu-ɔ. Na badubuti-ɔ baƒala kugyii lɛlɔ na baasɛ baduli ƒula lɛna bebweli kede kede.”

ref: Three days later, while the king was celebrating his birthday with a dinner for his officials, he sent for his personal servant and the chief cook.
text: Ɔƒɛni limeyi lɛtɛdi lɛmmɔ ni, lɛdi Ƒarao lɛwɔmeyi. Anwuɔsu ɔ́ya na bánwa adila na ɔ́lɔ nwuna benengu ƒui ka babadi limeyi bete-nwu. Na kasakɔ mmɔ ni, Ƒarao ɔ́ya na bákpanku ntɛ ku bodobodo lɛlɔ banune bammɔ bébenyene bana benengu bakladi bammɔ ƒui anumi.

ref: He put the personal servant back in his old job
text: Na ɔ́ya na ukyuli ɔni mɔɔnu-nwu ntɛ lɛlɔ ɔ́sɔ úsie úténye nwuna kabla kenye.

ref: and had the cook put to death. Everything happened just as Joseph had said it would,
text: Na bodobodo lɛlɔ ɔnune anwu ni, ɔ́ya na bákya-nwu ɔbuli ɔkwɛ báƒala ugyii lɛlɔ kalɛ kani na Yoseƒ údie ana ammɔ kalɔ úte-ma.

ref: Two years later the king of Egypt dreamed he was standing beside the Nile River.
text: Uwi ɔni na akwa anyɔ sɔɔn ávi ni, Ƒarao úgye kɔna kɔdi. Ɔ́nu na kɔna kemmo ka ni, anwu Ƒarao unye ketu bonwu kani baalɔ Nil kɔtu.

ref: Suddenly, seven fat, healthy cows came up from the river and started eating grass along the bank.
text: Mmɔ ni, ɔmantɛ bakɔ maatɛ badi bálɛ bási ketu kemmo bébenye-nwu anumi. Bebuo bammɔ bákɛ lɛlɔ na bélie lɛmwi kaduɛ, na benye mmɔ baakun livo lɛbuni lɛni nkya ketu kɔtu mmɔ.

ref: ate the fat, healthy cows. When this happened, the king woke up.
text: Bebuo bani nákwii mɛni béƒie bani nélie lɛmwi mmɔ bébweli. Na ka ɔ́nu lɛla lɛmɛni ni, Ƒarao úkeyi lɛlɔ ɔ́gyinya.

ref: The king went back to sleep and had another dream. This time seven full heads of grain were growing on a single stalk.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, lɛlɛɛ lɛ́sɔ líti-nwu. Na ka ɔ́lɛɛ ni, ɔsɔ úgye kɔna bamba. Na ɔƒɛni amu ni, ɔ́nu ka kamu liteli lɛ́kwɛ na kábwɛ eli maatɛ ku ebi buni.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: Na ebi abuni ammɔ kama ni, kamu liteli bamba lɛnɔ lɛ́kwɛ na kábwɛ eli maatɛ. Nsu anya ásiaku ɔƒɛ na áyɛyɛ, ɔsu ákya ebi ƒaƒaa.

ref: The thin heads of grain swallowed the seven full heads. Again the king woke up, and it had only been a dream.
text: Na ebi ƒaƒaa amɛni ébweli ebi abuni. Mmɔ ni, Ƒarao ɔ́sɔ ɔ́gyinya, na ɔ́bla-nwu nwaa ka kɔna kɔdi na úgye.

ref: The next morning the king was upset. So he called in his magicians and wise men and told them what he had dreamed. None of them could tell him what the dreams meant.
text: Na ɔtanta ɔmmɔ ni, kasusu kékpoku Ƒarao geen. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu ɔ́ya na bálɔ bekebone ku Egyipteƒuɔ banundane béte-nwu. Na ɔ́sɔku ana ani na úgye ƒui ɔ́su-ma. Nsu ɔdi útakana na udie-nya kalɔ ute-nwu.

ref: The king's personal servant said: Now I remember what I was supposed to do.
text: Ɔmmɔ kama ni, Ƒarao ntɛ lɛlɔ ɔnune unengu ɔmmɔ ɔ́su ɔnini, “Ɔmwi na lɛla lɛni mɔɔlɛ́ mɛni lɛ́ya na línyimo lɛla lɛni na ɔlɛka lɛbla, nsu ńtabla!

ref: When you were angry with me and your chief cook, you threw us both in jail in the house of the captain of the guard.
text: Uwi ɔdi úbavi ɔni na awɔ bula ati Ƒarao ákɛ kɔnyi ákya abu ƒula kabla bablane. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu bákya ami ku ƒula bodobodo ɔblane unengu ɔtɔ na ƒula bagyumanane ƒui unengu kesii.

ref: One night we both had dreams, and each dream had a different meaning.
text: Mmɔ ni, bula inyɔ ƒui búgye ana adi usien unwi kemmo, na kɔna biala ku lɛla lɛni na kɔdanku.

ref: A young Hebrew, who was a servant of the captain of the guard, was there with us at the time. When we told him our dreams, he explained what each of them meant,
text: Ɔƒɛni Hebrini okloƒu ɔdi lɛnɔ ɔ́kya-bu kudɔ mmɔ, na anwu lɛnɔ ɔtɛ ɔtɔ mmɔ na ɔɔbla ɔgyumanane ɔmmɔ kabla. Amu na bɔ́su-nwu bula ana ani na búgye. Na údie ɔdi biala kɔna kalɔ úte-nwu ku lɛla lɛni na ɔdi biala kɔdi kɔdanku.

ref: and everything happened just as he said it would. I got my job back, and the cook was put to death.
text: Na lɛla biala na ɔ́su ɔ́sia lɛni pɛpɛɛpɛ úte ɔdi biala. Ɔmmɔ ɔsu na lɛ́sɔ línwini líbo mina kabla kemmo na bádu ukyuli unyɔdi.”

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Amu ka Ƒarao únu lɛni ni, ɔ́ya na bátálɔ Yoseƒ béboku kɔnakunwi ɔ́siku ubu ɔni kemmo na bákya-nwu mmɔ. Na ɔ́kpi ntu na ɔ́kpɛ ukwi na ɔklibi lɛlɔ kaduɛ asa na ɔ́da úbalɛ Ƒarao anumi.

ref: The king said to him, “I had a dream, yet no one can explain what it means. I am told that you can interpret dreams.”
text: Mmɔ na Ƒarao ɔ́su Yoseƒ ɔnini, “Lígye kɔna kɔdi na ɔdi biala útakana-ku kalɔ budie. Nsu básu-mi ka awɔ aakana na eedie ana kalɔ si básu-ɔ kɔdi.”

ref: “Your Majesty,” Joseph answered, “I can't do it myself, but God can give a good meaning to your dreams.”
text: Amu na Yoseƒ údie kenye ɔnini, “Naaakana ana kalɔ budie, nsu lɛla lɛni na Katɔti ɔɔwia ni, ani na ɔdubɔsu Ƒarao.”

ref: The king told Joseph: I dreamed I was standing on the bank of the Nile River.
text: Mmɔ na Ƒarao úse kalɔ na ɔ́su Yoseƒ ɔnini, “Lígye kɔna ka nnye Nil Ketu kɔtu.

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: Na mmɔ ni, lɛ́nu ka ɔmantɛ bakɔ maatɛ bálɛ bási ketu kemmo mmɔ na bébomo na belie lɛmwi kaduɛ na benye baakun livo lɛbuni lɛni nwi ketu kɔtu mmɔ.

ref: Next, seven skinny, bony cows came up out of the river. I have never seen such terrible looking cows anywhere in Egypt.
text: Na bammɔ kama ni, maatɛ babambadi lɛnɔ básɔ bálɛ bási ketu kemmo mmɔ. Na bédubie na bákwii geen ɔni na baládia bɔnu. Na ńtanu bammɔ udu kudii na bula Egyipte kalɔ lɛlɔ mɛni kesii kadi kudii.

ref: But you couldn't tell it, because these skinny cows were just as skinny as they were before. At once, I woke up.
text: Nsu ɔmmɔ ƒui kama alanu bani nákwii mmɔ aláti anii bébweli lɛla lɛdi. Mmomu bámu bákwii benye lɛni kani. Mmɔ na lɛ́gyinya.”
